**Estonia**,[[b]](#cite_note-14) officially the **Republic of Estonia**,[[c]](#cite_note-15) is a country in the [Baltic region](/wiki/Baltic_region "Baltic region") of [Northern Europe](/wiki/Northern_Europe "Northern Europe").[[d]](#cite_note-location-20) It is bordered to the north by the [Gulf of Finland](/wiki/Gulf_of_Finland "Gulf of Finland") across from [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland"), to the west by the [Baltic Sea](/wiki/Baltic_Sea "Baltic Sea") across from [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden"), to the south by [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia "Latvia"), and to the east by [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"). The territory of Estonia consists of the [mainland](/wiki/Mainland "Mainland"), the larger islands of [Saaremaa](/wiki/Saaremaa "Saaremaa") and [Hiiumaa](/wiki/Hiiumaa "Hiiumaa"), and over 2,300 other islands and [islets](/wiki/Islet "Islet") on the east coast of the Baltic Sea.[[4]](#cite_note-ERR_area-5) Its capital city of [Tallinn](/wiki/Tallinn "Tallinn"), along with the city of [Tartu](/wiki/Tartu "Tartu"), are the country's two largest [urban areas](/wiki/List_of_cities_and_towns_in_Estonia "List of cities and towns in Estonia"). The [Estonian language](/wiki/Estonian_language "Estonian language"), of the [Finnic family](/wiki/Finnic_languages "Finnic languages"), is the [official language](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") and the [first language](/wiki/First_language "First language") of the [majority](/wiki/Estonians "Estonians") of nearly 1.4 million people. Estonia is one of the least populous [member states of the European Union](/wiki/Member_state_of_the_European_Union "Member state of the European Union").

Present-day Estonia has been inhabited since at least 9,000 BC. The [medieval indigenous](/wiki/Ancient_Estonia#Early_Middle_Ages "Ancient Estonia") population of Estonia was one of the last [pagan](/wiki/Pagan "Pagan") civilisations in Europe to adopt [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") following the [Northern Crusades](/wiki/Northern_Crusades "Northern Crusades") in the 13th century. After centuries of foreign rule by the [Teutonic Order](/wiki/State_of_the_Teutonic_Order "State of the Teutonic Order"), [Denmark](/wiki/Duchy_of_Estonia_(1219%E2%80%931346) "Duchy of Estonia (1219–1346)"), [Poland](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth"), [Sweden](/wiki/Estonia_under_Swedish_rule "Estonia under Swedish rule"), and the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire"), a distinct Estonian national identity gained new momentum with the [Age of Awakening](/wiki/Estonian_national_awakening "Estonian national awakening") in the mid-19th century. This culminated in the 1918 [Estonian Declaration of Independence](/wiki/Estonian_Declaration_of_Independence "Estonian Declaration of Independence"). Democratic throughout most of the [interwar period](/wiki/Interwar_period "Interwar period"), Estonia declared [neutrality](/wiki/Neutral_powers_during_World_War_II "Neutral powers during World War II") at the outbreak of [World War II](/wiki/Estonia_in_World_War_II "Estonia in World War II"), but the country was [repeatedly invaded and occupied](/wiki/Occupation_of_the_Baltic_states "Occupation of the Baltic states"), and ultimately [annexed](/wiki/Estonian_SSR "Estonian SSR") into the USSR. Throughout the *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* Soviet occupation, from World War II until 1991,[[17]](#cite_note-21) Estonia's *[de jure](/wiki/De_jure "De jure")* [state continuity](/wiki/State_continuity_of_the_Baltic_states "State continuity of the Baltic states") was [preserved](/wiki/Baltic_Legations_(1940%E2%80%931991) "Baltic Legations (1940–1991)") by [diplomatic representatives](/wiki/Estonian_Diplomatic_Service_(1940%E2%80%931991) "Estonian Diplomatic Service (1940–1991)") and the [government-in-exile](/wiki/Estonian_government-in-exile "Estonian government-in-exile"). Following the 1988–1990 "[Singing Revolution](/wiki/Singing_Revolution "Singing Revolution")" against Soviet rule, full independence was restored on [20 August 1991](/wiki/Estonian_Restoration_of_Independence "Estonian Restoration of Independence"), and the country joined [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") and the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") in 2004.

Estonia is a [developed country](/wiki/Developed_country "Developed country") with a [high-income](/wiki/World_Bank_high-income_economy "World Bank high-income economy"), advanced [economy](/wiki/Economy_of_Estonia "Economy of Estonia"), and [Eurozone](/wiki/Eurozone "Eurozone") membership. It is a democratic [unitary parliamentary republic](/wiki/Unitary_parliamentary_republic "Unitary parliamentary republic"), with a single-tier local government system consisting of 79 municipalities. Estonia is among the [least corrupt](/wiki/Corruption_Perceptions_Index#2020–2023 "Corruption Perceptions Index") countries in the world and ranks very high in international rankings for [education](/wiki/Education_Index "Education Index"),[[18]](#cite_note-22) [human development](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_Human_Development_Index "List of countries by Human Development Index"), [press freedom](/wiki/Press_Freedom_Index "Press Freedom Index"), online public services,[[19]](#cite_note-23)[[20]](#cite_note-24) and the prevalence of technology companies.[[21]](#cite_note-25)

## Name

Main article: [Name of Estonia](/wiki/Name_of_Estonia "Name of Estonia")

The name *Estonia* ([Estonian](/wiki/Estonian_language "Estonian language"): *Eesti* [[ˈeˑstʲi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Estonian "Help:IPA/Estonian") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Et-Eesti-female.oga "File:Et-Eesti-female.oga")) has been connected to the *[Aesti](/wiki/Aesti "Aesti")*, a people first mentioned by [Roman](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome") historian [Tacitus](/wiki/Tacitus "Tacitus") around AD 98. Some historians believe he was referring to the [Balts](/wiki/Balts "Balts"), while others think the name applied to all inhabitants of the eastern Baltic Sea region.[[22]](#cite_note-26) [Scandinavian sagas](/wiki/Scandinavian_sagas "Scandinavian sagas") and [Viking](/wiki/Viking "Viking") [runestones](/wiki/Viking_runestones "Viking runestones")[[23]](#cite_note-27) referring to *Eistland* are the earliest sources known to use the name in its modern geographic meaning.[[24]](#cite_note-28)

## History

Main article: [History of Estonia](/wiki/History_of_Estonia "History of Estonia")

### Prehistory

Main article: [Ancient Estonia](/wiki/Ancient_Estonia "Ancient Estonia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/StoneCistGraves.jpg/250px-StoneCistGraves.jpg)](/wiki/File:StoneCistGraves.jpg)

Bronze Age stone-cist graves at [Jõelähtme](/wiki/Rebala_Heritage_Reserve "Rebala Heritage Reserve")

The oldest known settlement in Estonia is the [Pulli settlement](/wiki/Pulli_settlement "Pulli settlement") that dates to the early 9th millennium BC, making it at least 11,000 years old.[[25]](#cite_note-Laurisar-29) The earliest human habitation during the [Mesolithic](/wiki/Mesolithic "Mesolithic") period is connected to the [Kunda culture](/wiki/Kunda_culture "Kunda culture").[[26]](#cite_note-30) [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") ceramics of the [Narva culture](/wiki/Narva_culture "Narva culture") appeared in around 5300 BC.[[27]](#cite_note-tautos-31)

The [Comb Ceramic culture](/wiki/Comb_Ceramic_culture "Comb Ceramic culture") emerged around 3900 BC. Its people, who were hunters and gatherers, lived near bodies of water. Raw materials like amber and flint were widely distributed through large trade networks. The culture also likely practiced religious beliefs, with animal and human figures made from bone and amber and deliberately shattered clay pieces, being found at settlement and burial sites.[[28]](#cite_note-comb-32)

The emergence of the [Corded Ware culture](/wiki/Corded_Ware_culture "Corded Ware culture"), which introduced primitive farming and animal husbandry, began approximately 2800 BC.[[29]](#cite_note-33) Archaeological estimates place the population within Estonian territory at c. 10,000 inhabitants in 2000 BC, growing from c. 6000 in around 3900 BC.[[28]](#cite_note-comb-32)

The first [hillfort](/wiki/Hillfort "Hillfort") settlements were established in the [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age").[[30]](#cite_note-34) A transition from hunter-fisher subsistence to single-farm-based settlement started around 1000 BC, and was complete by around 500 BC.[[31]](#cite_note-Laurisaar-35)[[32]](#cite_note-36) The large amount of bronze objects indicates active communication with Scandinavian and Germanic tribes.[[33]](#cite_note-37) During the [Iron Age](/wiki/Iron_Age_Europe "Iron Age Europe"), the local iron production began around 200 BC; north Estonia, particularly the coastal region of [Virumaa](/wiki/Virumaa "Virumaa"), emerged as a trade hub. North Estonian settlers gradually moved into nearby areas in the Baltic Sea region, spreading the [Proto-Finnic language](/wiki/Proto-Finnic_language "Proto-Finnic language").[[34]](#cite_note-eestijuured-38)

### The Viking Age

Main article: [Viking Age in Estonia](/wiki/Viking_Age_in_Estonia "Viking Age in Estonia")

The Estonian coast was a key trade hub situated on a major waterway, making it both a target and a starting point for numerous raids. Coastal Estonians, particularly the [Saaremaa islanders](/wiki/Oeselians "Oeselians"), adopted a [Viking](/wiki/Vikings "Vikings") lifestyle.[[35]](#cite_note-39)[[36]](#cite_note-40) Several Scandinavian sagas referred to major confrontations with Estonians, notably when 7th-century "Estonian Vikings" defeated and killed [Ingvar Harra](/wiki/Ingvar_Harra "Ingvar Harra"), the [King](/wiki/Yngling "Yngling") of the [Swedes](/wiki/Swedes_(tribe) "Swedes (tribe)").[[37]](#cite_note-41)[[38]](#cite_note-42) In c. 1030, Grand Prince [Yaroslav the Wise](/wiki/Yaroslav_the_Wise "Yaroslav the Wise") of [Kievan Rus'](/wiki/Kievan_Rus%27 "Kievan Rus'") attempted to subjugate the [Chuds](/wiki/Chud "Chud") (as East Slavic sources called Estonians and related Finnic tribes) in southeast Estonia and captured Tartu. Chuds (*Sosols*) destroyed this foothold in 1061.[[39]](#cite_note-43)[[40]](#cite_note-44)[[41]](#cite_note-45)[[42]](#cite_note-46) In 1187, Estonians, [Curonians](/wiki/Curonians "Curonians"), and [Karelians](/wiki/Karelians "Karelians") sacked [Sigtuna](/wiki/Sigtuna "Sigtuna"), a major Swedish city at the time.[[43]](#cite_note-47)[[44]](#cite_note-Tarvel-48)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Old_Estonian_counties.png/250px-Old_Estonian_counties.png)](/wiki/File:Old_Estonian_counties.png)

Pre-Christian independent Estonian counties (*maakond*), c. 1200

Estonia had two regional cultures in this period. Northern and western coastal areas maintained close connections with [Scandinavia](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia"), while the inland had stronger ties to the [Balts](/wiki/Balts "Balts") and the [principality of Pskov](/wiki/Pskov_Land "Pskov Land").[[45]](#cite_note-49) Ancient Estonia had a professional warrior caste,[[46]](#cite_note-50) while international trade provided nobles with wealth and prestige.[[47]](#cite_note-EestiajaluguI-51) This era's Estonian burial sites often contain both individual and collective graves, with artefacts like weapons and jewelry reflecting the shared material culture of Scandinavia and Northern Europe.[[48]](#cite_note-Magi2015-52)[[49]](#cite_note-53) Very little is known about the religious beliefs of medieval Estonians prior to [Christianisation](/wiki/Christianization "Christianization"). A 1229 [chronicle](/wiki/Chronicle_of_Henry_of_Livonia "Chronicle of Henry of Livonia") mentions *[Tharapita](/wiki/Tharapita "Tharapita")* as the supreme deity of the islanders of Saaremaa (Ösel). [Sacred groves](/wiki/Sacred_grove "Sacred grove"), particularly of [oak](/wiki/Oak "Oak") trees, factored prominently into pagan worship practices.[[50]](#cite_note-54)[[51]](#cite_note-55)

In the early centuries AD, Estonia's first administrative subdivisions emerged, primarily the parish (Estonian: *kihelkond*) and the county (Estonian: *[maakond](/wiki/Maakond "Maakond")*), usually including multiple parishes. Parishes were commonly centred on hill forts and ruled by local nobles.[[47]](#cite_note-EestiajaluguI-51) By the 13th century, Estonia was divided into eight major counties – [Harjumaa](/wiki/Harjumaa_(ancient_county) "Harjumaa (ancient county)"), [Järvamaa](/wiki/J%C3%A4rva_County "Järva County"), [Läänemaa](/wiki/L%C3%A4%C3%A4ne_County "Lääne County"), [Revala](/wiki/Revala "Revala"), [Saaremaa](/wiki/Saare_County "Saare County"), [Sakala](/wiki/Sakala_County "Sakala County"), [Ugandi](/wiki/Ugandi_County "Ugandi County"), and [Virumaa](/wiki/Virumaa "Virumaa") – as well as several smaller, single-parish counties. Counties operated independently, forming only loose defensive alliances against foreign threats.[[52]](#cite_note-56)[[53]](#cite_note-57)

### Christianisation and the Middle Ages

Main articles: [Livonian Crusade](/wiki/Livonian_Crusade "Livonian Crusade"), [Terra Mariana](/wiki/Terra_Mariana "Terra Mariana"), and [Danish Estonia](/wiki/Danish_Estonia "Danish Estonia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Medieval_Livonia_1260.svg/250px-Medieval_Livonia_1260.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Medieval_Livonia_1260.svg)

Estonia and Livonia after the 12th/13th century [Northern crusades](/wiki/Northern_crusades "Northern crusades")

In 1199, [Pope Innocent III](/wiki/Pope_Innocent_III "Pope Innocent III") declared a [crusade](/wiki/Livonian_Crusade "Livonian Crusade") to "defend the Christians of [Livonia](/wiki/Livonia "Livonia")".[[54]](#cite_note-58) The crusading German [Swordbrothers](/wiki/Livonian_Brothers_of_the_Sword "Livonian Brothers of the Sword"), who had previously subjugated [Livonians](/wiki/Livonians "Livonians"), [Latgalians](/wiki/Latgalians "Latgalians"), and [Selonians](/wiki/Selonians "Selonians"), started campaigning against Estonians in 1208. The following years saw many raids and counter-raids. In 1217, the Estonians suffered a significant defeat in the [battle](/wiki/Battle_of_St._Matthew%27s_Day "Battle of St. Matthew's Day") where their most prominent leader, [Lembitu](/wiki/Lembitu "Lembitu"), an elder of [Sakala](/wiki/Sakala_County "Sakala County"), was killed. In 1219, the armies of King [Valdemar II of Denmark](/wiki/Valdemar_II_of_Denmark "Valdemar II of Denmark") defeated Estonians in the [Battle of Lyndanisse](/wiki/Battle_of_Lyndanisse "Battle of Lyndanisse") (Tallinn), and conquered northern Estonia.[[55]](#cite_note-59)[[56]](#cite_note-60) In the uprising of 1223, Estonians were able to push the German and Danish invaders out of the whole country, except Tallinn. The crusaders soon resumed their offensive, and in 1227, Saaremaa was the last Estonian county to surrender and convert to Christianity.[[57]](#cite_note-61)[[58]](#cite_note-62)

In the 13th century, the newly Christian territory of present-day Estonia and Latvia was named [Terra Mariana](/wiki/Terra_Mariana "Terra Mariana"); later it became known simply as Livonia.[[59]](#cite_note-63) North Estonia became [part of](/wiki/Danish_Estonia "Danish Estonia") the [Kingdom of Denmark](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Denmark "Kingdom of Denmark"). The rest was divided between Swordbrothers and the [prince-bishops](/wiki/Prince-bishop "Prince-bishop") of [Dorpat](/wiki/Bishopric_of_Dorpat "Bishopric of Dorpat") and [Ösel–Wiek](/wiki/Bishopric_of_%C3%96sel%E2%80%93Wiek "Bishopric of Ösel–Wiek"). In 1236, the Swordbrothers merged into the then larger [Teutonic Order](/wiki/Teutonic_Order "Teutonic Order") (becoming its regional branch, the "[Livonian Order](/wiki/Livonian_Order "Livonian Order")").[[60]](#cite_note-64) In 1343, a major anti-German [uprising](/wiki/St._George%27s_Night_Uprising "St. George's Night Uprising") encompassed north Estonia and Saaremaa. The [Teutonic Order](/wiki/Teutonic_Order "Teutonic Order") suppressed the rebellion by 1345, and the next year bought the Estonian lands from the king of Denmark.[[61]](#cite_note-65)[[62]](#cite_note-66) The German upper-class minority consolidated its power after the unsuccessful rebellion.[[63]](#cite_note-67) For the subsequent centuries, [Low German](/wiki/Low_German "Low German") remained the language of the ruling elite in both Estonian cities and the countryside.[[64]](#cite_note-68)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/1282_L%C3%BCbeck_Law_codex_for_Tallinn.jpg/250px-1282_L%C3%BCbeck_Law_codex_for_Tallinn.jpg)](/wiki/File:1282_L%C3%BCbeck_Law_codex_for_Tallinn.jpg)

Lübeck Law Codex for Tallinn

[Tallinn](/wiki/Tallinn "Tallinn"), the capital of [Danish Estonia](/wiki/Duchy_of_Estonia_(1219%E2%80%931346) "Duchy of Estonia (1219–1346)"), founded on the site of Lindanise, adopted the [Lübeck law](/wiki/L%C3%BCbeck_law "Lübeck law") and received full town rights in 1248.[[65]](#cite_note-69) The [Hanseatic League](/wiki/Hanseatic_League "Hanseatic League") controlled trade on the Baltic Sea, and the four largest cities in Estonia became members: Tallinn, Tartu, [Pärnu](/wiki/P%C3%A4rnu "Pärnu"), and [Viljandi](/wiki/Viljandi "Viljandi").[[66]](#cite_note-70) Protected by stone walls and membership in the Hansa, prosperous cities like Tallinn and Tartu often defied other rulers of medieval Livonia.[[67]](#cite_note-71) In 1435, a treaty establishing the [Livonian Confederation](/wiki/Livonian_Confederation "Livonian Confederation") was signed by representatives of the Livonian Order, the bishoprics, and the major cities.[[68]](#cite_note-VLM-72)

The [Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation") began in central Europe in 1517, and soon spread northward to Livonia despite some opposition by the Livonian Order.[[69]](#cite_note-73) Towns were the first to embrace Protestantism in the 1520s, and by the 1530s, the majority of the landowners and rural population had adopted [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism").[[70]](#cite_note-Frucht121-74)[[71]](#cite_note-75) Church services were now conducted in the vernacular, which initially meant [Low German](/wiki/Low_German "Low German"), but from the 1530s onward, the regular religious services were held in Estonian.[[70]](#cite_note-Frucht121-74)[[72]](#cite_note-76)

### Swedish and Russian rule

Main articles: [Swedish Estonia](/wiki/Swedish_Estonia "Swedish Estonia"), [Governorate of Estonia](/wiki/Governorate_of_Estonia "Governorate of Estonia"), and [Governorate of Livonia](/wiki/Governorate_of_Livonia "Governorate of Livonia")

In 1558, Tsar [Ivan the Terrible](/wiki/Ivan_the_Terrible "Ivan the Terrible") of [Russia (Muscovy)](/wiki/Tsardom_of_Russia "Tsardom of Russia") invaded Livonia, starting the [Livonian War](/wiki/Livonian_War "Livonian War"). The Livonian Order was decisively [defeated](/wiki/Battle_of_Ergeme "Battle of Ergeme") in 1560. The majority of Livonia accepted Polish rule, while Reval and the nobles of Northern Estonia swore loyalty to the Swedish king, and the Bishop of Ösel-Wiek sold his lands to the Danish king. Russian forces gradually conquered the majority of Livonia, but in the late 1570s, the Polish-Lithuanian and Swedish armies started their own offensives, and the bloody war finally ended in 1583 with Russian defeat.[[73]](#cite_note-Hoover_Press-77)[[74]](#cite_note-78) As a result of the war, Northern Estonia became the Swedish [Duchy of Estonia](/wiki/Duchy_of_Estonia_(1561%E2%80%931721) "Duchy of Estonia (1561–1721)"), Southern Estonia became the Polish [Duchy of Livonia](/wiki/Duchy_of_Livonia "Duchy of Livonia"), and Saaremaa remained under Danish control.[[75]](#cite_note-79) [Wars](/wiki/List_of_wars_between_Poland_and_Sweden "List of wars between Poland and Sweden") between Sweden and Poland-Lithuania continued until 1629, when the victorious Sweden acquired south Estonia and northern Latvia.[[76]](#cite_note-80) Sweden [gained](/wiki/Second_Treaty_of_Br%C3%B6msebro_(1645) "Second Treaty of Brömsebro (1645)") Saaremaa from Denmark in 1645.[[77]](#cite_note-81) The wars cut the population of Estonia from about 250–270,000 people in the mid-16th century to 115–120,000 in the 1630s.[[78]](#cite_note-82)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Academia_Gustaviana.jpg/250px-Academia_Gustaviana.jpg)](/wiki/File:Academia_Gustaviana.jpg)

*Academia Gustaviana*, now the [University of Tartu](/wiki/University_of_Tartu "University of Tartu"), was founded in 1632 by King Gustavus Adolphus

The Swedish era in Estonia was marked by both religious repression and significant reforms. Initially, it brought Protestant [puritans](/wiki/Puritans "Puritans") who opposed traditional Estonian beliefs and practices, leading to [witch trials](/wiki/Witch_trials "Witch trials") and bans on [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music").[[79]](#cite_note-83) While large parts of the rural population remained in [serfdom](/wiki/Serfdom "Serfdom"), legal reforms under King [Charles XI of Sweden](/wiki/Charles_XI_of_Sweden "Charles XI of Sweden") strengthened both serfs' and [free tenant](/wiki/Free_tenant "Free tenant") farmers' land usage and inheritance rights, resulting in this period's reputation as "The Good Old Swedish Time" in historical memory.[[80]](#cite_note-84) King [Gustavus Adolphus](/wiki/Gustavus_Adolphus "Gustavus Adolphus") established [gymnasiums](/wiki/Gymnasium_(school) "Gymnasium (school)") in Tartu (which became the university in 1632) and Tallinn. [Printers](/wiki/Printer_(publishing) "Printer (publishing)") were established in both towns. The beginnings of the Estonian-language public education system appeared in the 1680s, largely owing to [Bengt Forselius](/wiki/Bengt_Gottfried_Forselius "Bengt Gottfried Forselius").[[81]](#cite_note-85) The population of Estonia grew rapidly until about 20% of the population died in the [Great Famine](/wiki/Great_Famine_of_Estonia "Great Famine of Estonia") of 1695–97.[[82]](#cite_note-86)

During the 1700–1721 [Great Northern War](/wiki/Great_Northern_War "Great Northern War"), the [Tsardom of Russia](/wiki/Tsardom_of_Russia "Tsardom of Russia") (Muscovy) conquered the whole of Estonia by 1710.[[83]](#cite_note-87) The war and [plague](/wiki/Great_Northern_War_plague_outbreak "Great Northern War plague outbreak") again devastated the population of Estonia, with the 1712 population estimated at only 150,000–170,000.[[84]](#cite_note-88) Russian administration restored all the political and landholding rights of Baltic Germans.[[85]](#cite_note-89) The rights of local farmers reached their lowest point, as serfdom completely dominated agricultural relations during the 18th century.[[86]](#cite_note-90) Tsarist regime sought to avoid conflicts with the local nobility, and the Baltic autonomous "special order" remained largely in effect until the late 19th century, marking a distinctive period of localised governance within the Russian Empire. Although serfdom had been abolished in Estonia in 1816–1819, major reforms improving farmers' rights started only in the mid-19th century.[[87]](#cite_note-91)

### National awakening and independence

Main articles: [Estonian national awakening](/wiki/Estonian_national_awakening "Estonian national awakening"), [Estonian Declaration of Independence](/wiki/Estonian_Declaration_of_Independence "Estonian Declaration of Independence"), and [History of Estonia § Interwar period (1920–1939)](/wiki/History_of_Estonia#Interwar_period_(1920–1939) "History of Estonia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Kreutzwald-k%C3%B6ler.jpg/250px-Kreutzwald-k%C3%B6ler.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kreutzwald-k%C3%B6ler.jpg)

[Friedrich Reinhold Kreutzwald](/wiki/Friedrich_Reinhold_Kreutzwald "Friedrich Reinhold Kreutzwald") (1803–1882), author of the Estonian [national epic](/wiki/National_epic "National epic") *[Kalevipoeg](/wiki/Kalevipoeg "Kalevipoeg")* (by [Johann Köler](/wiki/Johann_K%C3%B6ler "Johann Köler"), 1864)

The [Estonian national awakening](/wiki/Estonian_national_awakening "Estonian national awakening") began in the 1850s as several leading figures started promoting an Estonian national identity among the general populace. Widespread farm buyouts by Estonians and the resulting rapidly growing class of land-owning farmers provided the economic basis for the formation of this new "Estonian identity". In 1857 [Johann Voldemar Jannsen](/wiki/Johann_Voldemar_Jannsen "Johann Voldemar Jannsen") started publishing the first successful Estonian-language weekly newspaper, and began popularising the denomination of oneself as *eestlane* (Estonian).[[88]](#cite_note-EE286-92) The first nationwide movements formed, such as a campaign to establish an Estonian-language secondary school, the founding of the [Society of Estonian Literati](/wiki/Society_of_Estonian_Literati "Society of Estonian Literati") and the [Estonian Students' Society](/wiki/Estonian_Students%27_Society "Estonian Students' Society"), and the first [national song festival](/wiki/Estonian_Song_Festival "Estonian Song Festival"), held in 1869 in Tartu.[[89]](#cite_note-Raun59-93)[[90]](#cite_note-EE287-94)[[91]](#cite_note-95) The national [epic](/wiki/Epic_poetry "Epic poetry") *[Kalevipoeg](/wiki/Kalevipoeg "Kalevipoeg")* was published in 1862, and 1870 saw the first performances of [Estonian theatre](/wiki/Theatre_of_Estonia "Theatre of Estonia").[[92]](#cite_note-96)[[93]](#cite_note-CoE-97) In 1878 a major split happened in the national movement. The moderate wing led by [Jakob Hurt](/wiki/Jakob_Hurt "Jakob Hurt") focused on development of culture and Estonian education, while the radical wing led by [Carl Robert Jakobson](/wiki/Carl_Robert_Jakobson "Carl Robert Jakobson") started demanding increased political and economical rights.[[90]](#cite_note-EE287-94)

In the 1890s, the central government of the Russian Empire launched a [Russification](/wiki/Russification "Russification") campaign in order to tie the Baltic governorates more closely to the empire administratively and culturally.[[89]](#cite_note-Raun59-93) Russian language replaced German and Estonian in most secondary schools and universities, and many societal activities in local languages were suppressed.[[93]](#cite_note-CoE-97) In the late 1890s, prominent figures like [Jaan Tõnisson](/wiki/Jaan_T%C3%B5nisson "Jaan Tõnisson") and [Konstantin Päts](/wiki/Konstantin_P%C3%A4ts "Konstantin Päts") rose in a new surge of nationalism. In the early 20th century, Estonians started taking over their German-run town governments.[[94]](#cite_note-EE291-98)
During the [1905 Revolution](/wiki/1905_Russian_Revolution "1905 Russian Revolution"), the first legal Estonian political parties were founded. An Estonian national congress was convened and demanded the unification of Estonian areas into a single autonomous territory and an end to Russification. The unrest was accompanied by both peaceful [demonstrations](/wiki/Political_demonstration "Political demonstration") and violent [riots](/wiki/Riot "Riot"). The Tsarist government responded with a brutal crackdown; some 500 people were executed and hundreds more jailed or deported to Siberia.[[95]](#cite_note-99)[[96]](#cite_note-EE292-100)

In 1917, following the [collapse of the Russian Empire](/wiki/February_Revolution "February Revolution"), the [Russian Provisional Government](/wiki/Russian_Provisional_Government "Russian Provisional Government") yielded to popular demand and the two main Estonian-speaking governorates were merged into one [Autonomous Governorate of Estonia](/wiki/Autonomous_Governorate_of_Estonia "Autonomous Governorate of Estonia"), and the [Estonian Provincial Assembly](/wiki/Estonian_Provincial_Assembly "Estonian Provincial Assembly") was then elected.[[97]](#cite_note-101) [Bolsheviks](/wiki/Bolsheviks "Bolsheviks") [seized power](/wiki/Bolshevik_coup "Bolshevik coup") in Estonia in November 1917, and disbanded the Provincial Assembly. However, the Provincial Assembly established the [Salvation Committee](/wiki/Estonian_Salvation_Committee "Estonian Salvation Committee"), and during the short interlude between Russian retreat and [German arrival](/wiki/Operation_Faustschlag "Operation Faustschlag"), the committee [declared the independence of Estonia](/wiki/Estonian_Declaration_of_Independence "Estonian Declaration of Independence") on 24 February 1918, and formed the [Estonian Provisional Government](/wiki/Estonian_Provisional_Government "Estonian Provisional Government"). [German occupation](/wiki/German_occupation_of_Estonia_during_World_War_I "German occupation of Estonia during World War I") immediately followed, but after Germany's capitulation in World War I, they were forced to return power to the Estonian government on 19 November 1918.[[98]](#cite_note-102)[[99]](#cite_note-103)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Soomusrong_nr_2_Valgas_1919.jpg/250px-Soomusrong_nr_2_Valgas_1919.jpg)](/wiki/File:Soomusrong_nr_2_Valgas_1919.jpg)

Estonian armoured train during the war of independence

On 28 November 1918 [Soviet Russia](/wiki/Soviet_Russia "Soviet Russia") invaded, starting the [Estonian War of Independence](/wiki/Estonian_War_of_Independence "Estonian War of Independence").[[100]](#cite_note-104) The [Red Army](/wiki/Red_Army "Red Army") came within 30 km of Tallinn, but in January 1919, the [Estonian Army](/wiki/Estonian_Army "Estonian Army"), led by [Johan Laidoner](/wiki/Johan_Laidoner "Johan Laidoner"), went on a counter-offensive, ejecting Bolshevik forces from Estonia within a few months. Renewed Soviet attacks failed, and in spring, the Estonian army, in co-operation with [White Russian](/wiki/White_movement "White movement") forces, advanced into Russia and [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia "Latvia").[[101]](#cite_note-Pinder76-105)[[102]](#cite_note-106) In June 1919, Estonia [defeated](/wiki/Battle_of_C%C4%93sis_(1919) "Battle of Cēsis (1919)") the [German Landeswehr](/wiki/Baltische_Landeswehr "Baltische Landeswehr") which had attempted to dominate Latvia. The Red Army launched a [final offensive](/wiki/Battle_of_Krivasoo "Battle of Krivasoo") against [Narva](/wiki/Narva "Narva") in late 1919, but failed to achieve a breakthrough. On 2 February 1920, the [Tartu Peace Treaty](/wiki/Treaty_of_Tartu_(Russian%E2%80%93Estonian) "Treaty of Tartu (Russian–Estonian)") was signed by Estonia and Soviet Russia, with the latter pledging to permanently give up all sovereign claims to Estonia.[[101]](#cite_note-Pinder76-105)[[103]](#cite_note-107)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/EstConstAssmb.jpg/250px-EstConstAssmb.jpg)](/wiki/File:EstConstAssmb.jpg)

Estonian Constituent Assembly, opening session on 23 April 1919

In April 1919, the [Estonian Constituent Assembly](/wiki/Estonian_Constituent_Assembly "Estonian Constituent Assembly") was elected. They passed a sweeping [land reform](/wiki/Estonian_Land_Reform_of_1919 "Estonian Land Reform of 1919") and adopted a highly [liberal](/wiki/Liberalism "Liberalism") [constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Estonia "Constitution of Estonia"), establishing Estonia as a [parliamentary](/wiki/Riigikogu "Riigikogu") democracy.[[104]](#cite_note-108)[[105]](#cite_note-109) Estonia joined the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations") in 1921.[[106]](#cite_note-Ginneken-110) Estonia enjoyed rapid economic growth during the interwar period. Land reforms improved the farmers' conditions, but the country also prospered from industrialisation and the development of oil shale mining. With independence, most economic links with Russia dissolved, but trade rapidly reoriented towards Western markets.[[107]](#cite_note-111) Estonia's cultural-autonomy law for ethnic minorities, adopted in 1925, was one of the most liberal of its time.[[108]](#cite_note-112)

The [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") strained Estonia's political system, and in 1933 the right-wing [Vaps movement](/wiki/Vaps_movement "Vaps movement") spearheaded a constitutional reform establishing a strong presidency.[[109]](#cite_note-113)[[110]](#cite_note-114) On 12 March 1934 the acting head of state, [Konstantin Päts](/wiki/Konstantin_P%C3%A4ts "Konstantin Päts"), extended a state of emergency over the entire country, under the pretext that the Vaps movement had been planning a coup. Päts ruled by decree for several years while parliament did not reconvene ("[era of silence](/wiki/Era_of_silence "Era of silence")").[[111]](#cite_note-115) The Päts régime was relatively benign compared to other authoritarian régimes in [interwar](/wiki/Interwar_period "Interwar period") Europe, never using violence against political opponents.[[112]](#cite_note-116) A 1937 referendum adopted a new constitution. In 1938, an election including opposition candidates provided [a new bicameral parliament](/wiki/1938_Estonian_parliamentary_election "1938 Estonian parliamentary election").[[113]](#cite_note-117) Estonia signed non-aggression pacts [with the Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet%E2%80%93Estonian_Non-Aggression_Pact "Soviet–Estonian Non-Aggression Pact") in 1932, and [with Germany](/wiki/German%E2%80%93Estonian_Non-Aggression_Pact "German–Estonian Non-Aggression Pact") in 1939.[[114]](#cite_note-118) In 1939, Estonia declared neutrality, but this proved futile in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[115]](#cite_note-119)

### Occupations

Main articles: [Estonia in World War II](/wiki/Estonia_in_World_War_II "Estonia in World War II"), [Occupation of the Baltic states](/wiki/Occupation_of_the_Baltic_states "Occupation of the Baltic states"), and [Estonian SSR](/wiki/Estonian_SSR "Estonian SSR")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/PlaqueMemorizingEstonianGovernment.jpg/250px-PlaqueMemorizingEstonianGovernment.jpg)](/wiki/File:PlaqueMemorizingEstonianGovernment.jpg)

Ten former Estonian prime ministers and 56 government members were executed or perished in captivity of the Soviet occupation regime after 1940 (memorial plaque at the [seat of government](/wiki/Stenbock_House "Stenbock House") in [Toompea](/wiki/Toompea "Toompea"), Tallinn)

A week before the outbreak of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the 23 August 1939 [Nazi-Soviet Pact](/wiki/Molotov%E2%80%93Ribbentrop_Pact "Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact") secretly assigned Estonia to the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union")'s sphere of influence.[[116]](#cite_note-120) In September 1939, during the [Soviet invasion of Poland](/wiki/Soviet_invasion_of_Poland "Soviet invasion of Poland"), [Joseph Stalin](/wiki/Joseph_Stalin "Joseph Stalin") pressured the Estonian government into signing a "[mutual assistance treaty](/wiki/Soviet%E2%80%93Estonian_Mutual_Assistance_Treaty "Soviet–Estonian Mutual Assistance Treaty")", allowing the USSR to establish military bases in Estonia.[[117]](#cite_note-121) On 14 June 1940, the Soviet Union instituted a full naval and air blockade on Estonia, and on 16 June, demanded free passage of the [Red Army](/wiki/Red_Army "Red Army") into Estonia and the establishment of a pro-Soviet government. Feeling that resistance was hopeless, the Estonian government complied and Soviet occupation began.[[118]](#cite_note-122)[[119]](#cite_note-123) On 6 August 1940, Estonia was formally annexed by the Soviet Union as the [Estonian SSR](/wiki/Estonian_SSR "Estonian SSR").[[120]](#cite_note-malksoo-124) The USSR established a repressive terror regime in occupied Estonia, targeting the country's elite for destruction. Hundreds of people were executed and, on 14 June 1941, c. 11,000 Estonians were [deported to Russia](/wiki/June_deportation "June deportation"), where most would be killed.[[121]](#cite_note-Miljan110-125)[[122]](#cite_note-126)

When Germany launched [Operation Barbarossa](/wiki/Operation_Barbarossa "Operation Barbarossa") against the Soviet Union on 22 June, the [Summer War](/wiki/Summer_War "Summer War") began in Estonia. The Soviet authorities conscripted around 34,000 young Estonian men; fewer than 30% would survive the war. Soviet [extermination battalions](/wiki/Extermination_battalion "Extermination battalion") adopted a [scorched-earth policy](/wiki/Scorched-earth_policy "Scorched-earth policy"), [massacring civilians](/wiki/Battle_of_Kautla "Battle of Kautla") in the process, and [NKVD](/wiki/NKVD "NKVD") units [executed political prisoners](/wiki/NKVD_prisoner_massacres "NKVD prisoner massacres") who could not be evacuated.[[123]](#cite_note-127)[[124]](#cite_note-128) Thousands of Estonians joined anti-Soviet partisan groups known as the [Forest Brothers](/wiki/Forest_Brothers "Forest Brothers").[[125]](#cite_note-129) The Soviets [evacuated Tallinn by sea](/wiki/Soviet_evacuation_of_Tallinn "Soviet evacuation of Tallinn") in late August 1941, suffering massive losses in the process.[[126]](#cite_note-pinder80-130) A puppet [Estonian Self-Administration](/wiki/Estonian_Self-Administration "Estonian Self-Administration") was established, and [occupied Estonia](/wiki/German_occupation_of_Estonia_during_World_War_II "German occupation of Estonia during World War II") was merged into [Reichskommissariat Ostland](/wiki/Reichskommissariat_Ostland "Reichskommissariat Ostland").[[127]](#cite_note-131) About a thousand [Estonian Jews](/wiki/Estonian_Jews "Estonian Jews") were [killed](/wiki/Holocaust_in_Estonia "Holocaust in Estonia") in 1941 and numerous forced labour camps were established.[[128]](#cite_note-CommissionReport-132) German occupation authorities initially started recruiting men into [volunteer units](/wiki/Estonian_Legion "Estonian Legion"), but forced conscription was instituted in 1943, eventually leading to formation of the [Estonian Waffen-SS division](/wiki/20th_Waffen_Grenadier_Division_of_the_SS_(1st_Estonian) "20th Waffen Grenadier Division of the SS (1st Estonian)").[[129]](#cite_note-133) Thousands of Estonians escaped to Finland, where [many volunteered](/wiki/Finnish_Infantry_Regiment_200 "Finnish Infantry Regiment 200") to fight together with Finns [against Soviets](/wiki/Continuation_War "Continuation War").[[130]](#cite_note-134)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Tallinn_Old_Town_after_being_bombed_by_the_Soviet_Air_Forces_in_1944.jpg/250px-Tallinn_Old_Town_after_being_bombed_by_the_Soviet_Air_Forces_in_1944.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tallinn_Old_Town_after_being_bombed_by_the_Soviet_Air_Forces_in_1944.jpg)

[Tallinn Old Town](/wiki/Tallinn_Old_Town "Tallinn Old Town") after [bombing by the Soviet air force](/wiki/Bombing_of_Tallinn_in_World_War_II "Bombing of Tallinn in World War II") (March 1944)

The Red Army reached the Estonian borders again in early 1944, but its advance into Estonia was stopped in [heavy fighting near Narva](/wiki/Battle_of_Narva_(1944) "Battle of Narva (1944)") for six months by German forces, including numerous Estonian units.[[131]](#cite_note-Raun159-135) In March, the Soviet Air Force carried out heavy bombing raids [against Tallinn](/wiki/Bombing_of_Tallinn_in_World_War_II "Bombing of Tallinn in World War II") and other Estonian towns.[[132]](#cite_note-136) In July, the Soviets started [a major offensive](/wiki/Tallinn_Offensive "Tallinn Offensive") from the south, forcing the Germans to abandon mainland Estonia in September, with the Estonian islands being abandoned in November.[[131]](#cite_note-Raun159-135) As German forces were retreating from Tallinn, the last pre-war prime minister [Jüri Uluots](/wiki/J%C3%BCri_Uluots "Jüri Uluots") [appointed a government](/wiki/National_Committee_of_the_Republic_of_Estonia "National Committee of the Republic of Estonia") headed by [Otto Tief](/wiki/Otto_Tief "Otto Tief") in an unsuccessful attempt to restore Estonia's independence.[[133]](#cite_note-137) Tens of thousands of Estonians fled westwards from the Soviets.[[134]](#cite_note-138) Estonia lost around one fourth of its population through war-related deaths, deportations and evacuations.[[135]](#cite_note-139)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Estonian_forest_brothers_relaxing_and_cleaning_their_guns_after_a_shooting_exercise_in_Veskiaru%2C_J%C3%A4rva_County%2C_Estonia%2C_1953._%2847953893422%29.jpg/250px-Estonian_forest_brothers_relaxing_and_cleaning_their_guns_after_a_shooting_exercise_in_Veskiaru%2C_J%C3%A4rva_County%2C_Estonia%2C_1953._%2847953893422%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Estonian_forest_brothers_relaxing_and_cleaning_their_guns_after_a_shooting_exercise_in_Veskiaru,_J%C3%A4rva_County,_Estonia,_1953._(47953893422).jpg)

Estonian [Anti-Soviet resistance fighters](/wiki/Forest_Brothers "Forest Brothers") after a shooting exercise in 1953

The United States, United Kingdom, France, Germany, and the majority of other Western countries [considered](/wiki/Stimson_Doctrine "Stimson Doctrine") the annexation of Estonia by the Soviet Union illegal.[[136]](#cite_note-140) [Legal continuity](/wiki/State_continuity_of_the_Baltic_states "State continuity of the Baltic states") of the Estonian state was preserved through the [government-in-exile](/wiki/Estonian_government-in-exile "Estonian government-in-exile") and the Estonian [diplomatic representatives](/wiki/Estonian_Diplomatic_Service_(1940%E2%80%9391) "Estonian Diplomatic Service (1940–91)") which Western governments continued to recognise.[[137]](#cite_note-141)[[138]](#cite_note-142) Thousands of Estonians opposing the second Soviet occupation joined a guerrilla movement known as the "[Forest Brothers](/wiki/Estonian_partisans "Estonian partisans")". The armed resistance was heaviest in the first few years after the war, but Soviet authorities gradually wore it down through attrition, and resistance effectively ceased to exist in the mid-1950s.[[139]](#cite_note-143) The Soviets initiated a [policy of collectivisation](/wiki/Collectivization_in_the_Soviet_Union "Collectivization in the Soviet Union"), but as farmers remained opposed to it a campaign of terror was unleashed. In March 1949 about 20,000 Estonians [were deported](/wiki/Operation_Priboi "Operation Priboi") to Siberia. Collectivization was fully completed soon afterwards.[[121]](#cite_note-Miljan110-125)[[140]](#cite_note-144)

The Soviet Union began [Russification](/wiki/Russification "Russification"), with hundreds of thousands of [Russians](/wiki/Russians_in_Estonia "Russians in Estonia") and people of other [Soviet](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") nationalities being induced to settle in Estonia, which eventually threatened to turn Estonians into a minority in their own land.[[141]](#cite_note-Taagepera97-145) In 1945 Estonians formed 97% of the population, but by 1989 their share of the population had fallen to 62%.[[142]](#cite_note-146) The military presence was pervasive, with closed military zones occupying around one-fifth of the Estonian land and the entire surrounding sea. Access to coastal areas required permits, rendering the Estonian people physically isolated from the world outside the USSR.[[143]](#cite_note-147)[[144]](#cite_note-148) Economically, heavy industry was strongly prioritised, but this did not improve the well-being of the local population, and caused massive environmental damage through pollution.[[145]](#cite_note-149) Living standards under the Soviet occupation kept falling further behind nearby independent Finland.[[141]](#cite_note-Taagepera97-145)

### Independence restored

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Balti_kett_22.jpg/250px-Balti_kett_22.jpg)](/wiki/File:Balti_kett_22.jpg)

In the [Baltic Way](/wiki/Baltic_Way "Baltic Way") on 23 August 1989, two million people formed a human chain across three countries in a mass demonstration against the Soviet occupation.

The introduction of *[perestroika](/wiki/Perestroika "Perestroika")* by the Soviet government in 1987 enabled political activism in Estonia, sparking the [Singing Revolution](/wiki/Singing_Revolution "Singing Revolution"), a peaceful movement towards independence.[[146]](#cite_note-150) One of the first major acts of resistance was the [Phosphorite War](/wiki/Phosphorite_War "Phosphorite War"), an environmental protest against Soviet plans to establish large phosphate mines in [Virumaa](/wiki/Virumaa "Virumaa").[[147]](#cite_note-151) On 23 August 1987, the [Hirvepark meeting](/wiki/Hirvepark_meeting "Hirvepark meeting") in Tallinn called for the public disclosure of the secret protocols of the Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact that had led to Estonia's occupation.[[148]](#cite_note-152)[[149]](#cite_note-153) In 1988, new political movements emerged, including the [Popular Front of Estonia](/wiki/Popular_Front_of_Estonia "Popular Front of Estonia"), a moderate faction within the independence movement, and the [Estonian National Independence Party](/wiki/Estonian_National_Independence_Party "Estonian National Independence Party"), which became the first non-communist political party registered in the Soviet Union.[[150]](#cite_note-154) The [parliament of Soviet-controlled Estonia](/wiki/Supreme_Soviet_of_the_Estonian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic "Supreme Soviet of the Estonian Soviet Socialist Republic") asserted the primacy of Estonian laws with the [Sovereignty Declaration](/wiki/Estonian_Sovereignty_Declaration "Estonian Sovereignty Declaration") on 16 November 1988, inspiring similar declarations across other [Soviet republics](/wiki/Republics_of_the_Soviet_Union "Republics of the Soviet Union").[[151]](#cite_note-155)[[152]](#cite_note-156) On 23 August 1989, two million people formed the [Baltic Way](/wiki/Baltic_Way "Baltic Way"), a human chain spanning Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania, to demonstrate unity in pursuit of independence.[[153]](#cite_note-157)

In 1990, the [Congress of Estonia](/wiki/Congress_of_Estonia "Congress of Estonia") was formed as the representative body of Estonian citizens.[[154]](#cite_note-158) In March 1991, a [referendum was held](/wiki/1991_Estonian_independence_referendum "1991 Estonian independence referendum") where 78.4% of voters supported independence, and during the [coup attempt in Moscow](/wiki/1991_Soviet_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat_attempt "1991 Soviet coup d'état attempt"), Estonia [declared restoration of independence](/wiki/Estonian_restoration_of_Independence "Estonian restoration of Independence") on 20 August.[[155]](#cite_note-159) Soviet authorities recognised Estonian independence on 6 September, and on 17 September Estonia was admitted into the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations").[[156]](#cite_note-Dillon164-160) The last units of the [Russian army](/wiki/Russian_army "Russian army") left Estonia in 1994.[[157]](#cite_note-161) In 1992 radical economic reforms were launched for switching over to a market economy, including privatisation and currency reform.[[158]](#cite_note-162) Estonian foreign policy since independence has been oriented toward [the West](/wiki/Western_world "Western world"), and in 2004 Estonia joined both the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") and [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO").[[159]](#cite_note-163) In 2011, Estonia joined the [eurozone](/wiki/Eurozone "Eurozone") and adopted the EU single currency.[[160]](#cite_note-164)

## Geography

Main article: [Geography of Estonia](/wiki/Geography_of_Estonia "Geography of Estonia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Ontika_maastikukaitseala.jpg/250px-Ontika_maastikukaitseala.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ontika_maastikukaitseala.jpg)

The cliff at Valaste Falls illustrates the stratigraphy of various geological eras

Estonia is [geographically](/wiki/Geography_of_Estonia "Geography of Estonia") located in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"),[[d]](#cite_note-location-20) on the eastern shores of the Baltic Sea, on the [East European Plain](/wiki/East_European_Plain "East European Plain").[[161]](#cite_note-Raukas_2018-165)[[162]](#cite_note-EEA-166)[[163]](#cite_note-167) It is located on the northwestern part of the [East European Platform](/wiki/East_European_Platform "East European Platform"), bordering the [Fennoscandian Shield](/wiki/Fennoscandian_Shield "Fennoscandian Shield").[[164]](#cite_note-168) It covers an area of 45,335 km2 (17,504 sq mi), of which 9.2% are islands and 4.6% is internal waters.[[161]](#cite_note-Raukas_2018-165)

The Estonian coastline stretches for 3,794 km (2,357 mi) and features [limestone cliffs](/wiki/Cliffed_coast "Cliffed coast") along the northern coast as well as its largest islands.[[162]](#cite_note-EEA-166)[[165]](#cite_note-169) Estonia includes 2,355 [islands](/wiki/List_of_islands_of_Estonia "List of islands of Estonia"), counting those in internal waters, with 2,222 in the Baltic Sea. The largest islands are [Saaremaa](/wiki/Saaremaa "Saaremaa") and [Hiiumaa](/wiki/Hiiumaa "Hiiumaa").[[161]](#cite_note-Raukas_2018-165) The country's terrain is predominantly flat, with an average elevation of about 50 metres (164 ft) above sea level. While plains dominate in the north and west, the south and east of Estonia are hillier. [Suur Munamägi](/wiki/Suur_Munam%C3%A4gi "Suur Munamägi"), the highest peak of the country at 318 metres (1,043 ft), is situated in the [Haanja Upland](/wiki/Haanja_Upland "Haanja Upland").[[161]](#cite_note-Raukas_2018-165)

There are c. 1,560 natural [lakes in Estonia](/wiki/List_of_lakes_of_Estonia "List of lakes of Estonia"); [Lake Peipus](/wiki/Lake_Peipus "Lake Peipus") and [Võrtsjärv](/wiki/V%C3%B5rtsj%C3%A4rv "Võrtsjärv") are the two largest. There are more than [7,000 rivers](/wiki/List_of_rivers_of_Estonia "List of rivers of Estonia"), streams, and canals, with only ten of them exceeding 100 km (62 mi) in length. The longest rivers in Estonia include the [Võhandu](/wiki/V%C3%B5handu "Võhandu") at 162 km (101 mi) and the [Pärnu](/wiki/P%C3%A4rnu_(river) "Pärnu (river)") at 144 km (89 mi). The rivers are primarily fed by groundwater, rainfall, and snowmelt, with each source contributing approximately one-third of the annual runoff. [Bogs](/wiki/Bogs "Bogs") and [mires](/wiki/Mire "Mire") cover about 23% of the country.[[161]](#cite_note-Raukas_2018-165)

### Climate

Estonia has a transitional [climate](/wiki/Climate_of_Estonia "Climate of Estonia") that lies between [continental](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification#Group_D:_Continental/microthermal_climates "Köppen climate classification") and [maritime](/wiki/Maritime_climate "Maritime climate") influences, characterised as a humid continental climate. Estonia's climate is notably milder than that of other regions at the same latitude due to the moderating effects of the [North Atlantic Current](/wiki/North_Atlantic_Current "North Atlantic Current"). The prevailing weather patterns in Estonia are significantly influenced by active cyclonic activity in the northern [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean"). This results in strong winds, precipitation, and abrupt temperature fluctuations, especially during the autumn and winter months. The westerly winds carry moist maritime air far into continental interior, leading to milder temperatures in winter and cooler conditions in summer compared to the continental areas further away from the coast. Coastal regions and islands generally enjoy a milder climate, as the Baltic Sea moderates temperatures, keeping coastal areas warmer in winter and cooler in summer.[[166]](#cite_note-estonica_climate-170)

The calculated mean temperature for the country's territory ranges from 17.8 °C (64.0 °F) in July to −3.8 °C (25.2 °F) in February, with the annual average being 6.4 °C (43.5 °F).[[167]](#cite_note-171) The highest recorded temperature is 35.6 °C (96.1 °F) from 1992, although the unofficial record high temperature is 38.0 °C (104.8 °F) from the same year[[168]](#cite_note-172) and the lowest is −43.5 °C (−46.3 °F) from 1940.[[169]](#cite_note-173) The annual average precipitation is 662 millimetres (26.1 in),[[170]](#cite_note-174) with the daily record being 148 millimetres (5.8 in).[[171]](#cite_note-175) Snow cover varies significantly on different years. Prevailing winds are westerly, southwesterly, and southerly, the average annual wind speed varies between 2.1 m/s and 6.1 m/s, being smaller inland and the largest on the west coast.[[162]](#cite_note-EEA-166) Due to high latitude, day length varies significantly between summer and winter. In Tallinn, the longest day of the year lasts up to 18 hours and 40 minutes, while the shortest day lasts only 6 hours and 2 minutes.[[166]](#cite_note-estonica_climate-170) The average monthly sunshine duration ranges from 290 hours in August, to 21 hours in December.[[172]](#cite_note-176) As a calculated average, Estonia receives 1830 hours of sunshine per year.[[173]](#cite_note-177)

### Biodiversity

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Korsapalo_2013_s%C3%BCgis.JPG/250px-Korsapalo_2013_s%C3%BCgis.JPG)](/wiki/File:Korsapalo_2013_s%C3%BCgis.JPG)

Scots pine *[Pinus sylvestris](/wiki/Pinus_sylvestris "Pinus sylvestris")* is the most common tree species in Estonian forests.

Estonia is recognised as one of the most biodiverse regions in Europe, particularly for its size and latitude.[[162]](#cite_note-EEA-166) The country boasts a diverse array of climatic and soil conditions, as well as an abundance of both marine and freshwater ecosystems. This rich biodiversity allows for the survival of many species that have become extinct in most other European nations.[[174]](#cite_note-Taylor_1-178) [Protected areas of Estonia](/wiki/Protected_areas_of_Estonia "Protected areas of Estonia") cover 19.4% of the land (or 23% of the total area incl. territorial sea). The country is home to nearly 4,000 protected natural objects including 6 national parks, 231 nature conservation areas, and 154 landscape reserves.[[175]](#cite_note-179)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Mustela_lutreola-cropped.jpg/250px-Mustela_lutreola-cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mustela_lutreola-cropped.jpg)

Estonia is one of the few countries where critically endangered [European mink](/wiki/European_mink "European mink") is present in the wild

Estonian fauna has a significant presence of aquatic, riparian, forest, and open-field species. The country is home to 64 recorded [mammal](/wiki/Mammal "Mammal") species, 11 [amphibians](/wiki/Amphibian "Amphibian"), and 5 [reptiles](/wiki/Reptile "Reptile").[[161]](#cite_note-Raukas_2018-165) Large mammals include the [grey wolf](/wiki/Eurasian_wolf "Eurasian wolf"), [lynx](/wiki/Eurasian_lynx "Eurasian lynx"), [brown bear](/wiki/Brown_bear "Brown bear"), [red fox](/wiki/Red_fox "Red fox"), [badger](/wiki/European_badger "European badger"), [wild boar](/wiki/Wild_boar "Wild boar"), [moose](/wiki/Moose "Moose"), [roe deer](/wiki/Roe_deer "Roe deer"), [beaver](/wiki/Eurasian_beaver "Eurasian beaver"), [otter](/wiki/Eurasian_otter "Eurasian otter"), [grey seal](/wiki/Grey_seal "Grey seal"), and [ringed seal](/wiki/Ringed_seal "Ringed seal"). The critically endangered European mink has been successfully reintroduced on [Hiiumaa](/wiki/Hiiumaa "Hiiumaa"), while the rare [Siberian flying squirrel](/wiki/Siberian_flying_squirrel "Siberian flying squirrel") thrives in eastern Estonia.[[174]](#cite_note-Taylor_1-178) [Red deer](/wiki/Red_deer "Red deer"), previously extirpated, have been successfully reintroduced.[[176]](#cite_note-180) In the early 21st century, a population of [European jackals](/wiki/European_jackal "European jackal") was confirmed in western Estonia, expanding their range significantly.[[177]](#cite_note-181)[[178]](#cite_note-182) Introduced mammals include [sika deer](/wiki/Sika_deer "Sika deer"), [fallow deer](/wiki/European_fallow_deer "European fallow deer"), [raccoon dog](/wiki/Common_raccoon_dog "Common raccoon dog"), [muskrat](/wiki/Muskrat "Muskrat"), and American mink.[[161]](#cite_note-Raukas_2018-165) Over 330 bird species have been found in Estonia, including the [white-tailed eagle](/wiki/White-tailed_eagle "White-tailed eagle"), [lesser spotted eagle](/wiki/Lesser_spotted_eagle "Lesser spotted eagle"), [golden eagle](/wiki/Golden_eagle "Golden eagle"), [western capercaillie](/wiki/Western_capercaillie "Western capercaillie"), [black](/wiki/Black_Stork "Black Stork") and [white stork](/wiki/White_stork "White stork"), and a variety of [owls](/wiki/Owls "Owls"), [waders](/wiki/Wader "Wader"), and [geese](/wiki/Geese "Geese").[[179]](#cite_note-Taylor_2-183) The [barn swallow](/wiki/Barn_swallow "Barn swallow") is the [national bird](/wiki/National_bird "National bird") of Estonia.[[180]](#cite_note-184)

[Phytogeographically](/wiki/Phytogeography "Phytogeography"), Estonia is shared between the Central European and Eastern European provinces of the [Circumboreal Region](/wiki/Circumboreal_Region "Circumboreal Region") within the [Boreal Kingdom](/wiki/Boreal_Kingdom "Boreal Kingdom"). According to the [WWF](/wiki/World_Wide_Fund_for_Nature "World Wide Fund for Nature"), the territory of Estonia belongs to the [ecoregion](/wiki/Ecoregion "Ecoregion") of [Sarmatic mixed forests](/wiki/Sarmatic_mixed_forests "Sarmatic mixed forests").[[181]](#cite_note-DinersteinOlson2017-185) The country has a rich composition of floristic groups, with estimated 3,000 [algae](/wiki/Algae "Algae") and [cyanobacteria](/wiki/Cyanobacteria "Cyanobacteria") species, 850 [lichens](/wiki/Lichens "Lichens"), and 600 [bryophytes](/wiki/Bryophytes "Bryophytes"). As of 2023, forests covered 51.5% of Estonia's land area, supporting a wide range of plant species.[[182]](#cite_note-186) Among these, 87 native and over 500 introduced tree and bush species have been identified, with the most common trees being [pine](/wiki/Pine "Pine") (41%), [birch](/wiki/Birch "Birch") (28%), and [spruce](/wiki/Spruce "Spruce") (23%).[[161]](#cite_note-Raukas_2018-165) [Saaremaa yellow rattle](/wiki/Rhinanthus_osiliensis "Rhinanthus osiliensis") is [endemic](/wiki/Endemism "Endemism") to [Saaremaa](/wiki/Saaremaa "Saaremaa") island and cannot be found elsewhere in the world.[[183]](#cite_note-187) The national flower of Estonia is the [cornflower](/wiki/Centaurea_cyanus "Centaurea cyanus") (*Centaurea cyanus*).[[184]](#cite_note-Cyanus-188)

## Government and politics

Main articles: [Politics of Estonia](/wiki/Politics_of_Estonia "Politics of Estonia"), [List of political parties in Estonia](/wiki/List_of_political_parties_in_Estonia "List of political parties in Estonia"), and [Elections in Estonia](/wiki/Elections_in_Estonia "Elections in Estonia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Alar_Karis_2024.jpg/250px-Alar_Karis_2024.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alar_Karis_2024.jpg)

[Alar Karis](/wiki/Alar_Karis "Alar Karis")  
[President](/wiki/President_of_Estonia "President of Estonia") since 2021

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Kristen_Michal_in_2024_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Kristen_Michal_in_2024_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kristen_Michal_in_2024_(cropped).jpg)

[Kristen Michal](/wiki/Kristen_Michal "Kristen Michal")  
[Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Estonia "Prime Minister of Estonia") since 2024

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Riigikogu_hoone%2C_Kaupo_Kalda_foto%2C_2016.jpg/250px-Riigikogu_hoone%2C_Kaupo_Kalda_foto%2C_2016.jpg)](/wiki/File:Riigikogu_hoone,_Kaupo_Kalda_foto,_2016.jpg)

The seat of the Parliament of Estonia in Toompea Castle

Estonia is a unitary [parliamentary republic](/wiki/Parliamentary_republic "Parliamentary republic") where the unicameral parliament, [Riigikogu](/wiki/Riigikogu "Riigikogu"), serves as the legislature and [the government](/wiki/Government_of_Estonia "Government of Estonia") acts as the executive branch.[[185]](#cite_note-Toots-189) The Riigikogu comprises 101 members elected for four-year terms by [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation"), with voting rights granted to citizens over 18 years of age. The parliament approves the national government, passes legal acts and the state budget, and exercises parliamentary oversight. Additionally, upon [the president](/wiki/President_of_Estonia "President of Estonia")'s recommendation, the Parliament appoints the Chief Justice of the Supreme Court, the chair of the Bank of Estonia, the Auditor General, the Chancellor of Justice, and the Commander-in-Chief of the Defence Forces.[[186]](#cite_note-190)[[187]](#cite_note-191)

The [Government of Estonia](/wiki/Government_of_Estonia "Government of Estonia"), led by the [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Estonia "Prime Minister of Estonia"), is nominated by the President, subject to parliamentary approval. Estonia's political system has been marked by coalition governments, as no single party has managed to secure an absolute majority in parliament.[[185]](#cite_note-Toots-189) The President, Estonia's head of state, plays a mostly ceremonial role, representing the nation internationally and holding the power to proclaim or veto laws passed by the Parliament. Should a law be passed unamended after presidential veto, the President may petition the Supreme Court to review its constitutionality.[[185]](#cite_note-Toots-189)[[188]](#cite_note-192) The President is elected by the Riigikogu, or by a special electoral college.[[189]](#cite_note-193)

The [Constitution of Estonia](/wiki/Constitution_of_Estonia "Constitution of Estonia") allows [referendums](/wiki/Referendum "Referendum"). After the adoption of the current constitution by a referendum in 1992, only one more referendum has been held: the [2003 Estonian European Union membership referendum](/wiki/2003_Estonian_European_Union_membership_referendum "2003 Estonian European Union membership referendum").[[190]](#cite_note-194) Estonia has pioneered in [e-government](/wiki/E-government "E-government"), offering nearly all public services online[[191]](#cite_note-195) and becoming the first country globally to enable [nationwide binding Internet voting](/wiki/Electronic_voting_in_Estonia "Electronic voting in Estonia") in 2005 local elections.[[192]](#cite_note-196) During the [2023 parliamentary elections](/wiki/2023_Estonian_parliamentary_election "2023 Estonian parliamentary election"), over half of the votes were cast online.[[193]](#cite_note-197) Six parties secured seats in the Riigikogu in the 2023 elections, with [Kaja Kallas](/wiki/Kaja_Kallas "Kaja Kallas") of the [Reform Party](/wiki/Estonian_Reform_Party "Estonian Reform Party") forming a [coalition government](/wiki/Kaja_Kallas%27_third_cabinet "Kaja Kallas' third cabinet") with [Estonia 200](/wiki/Estonia_200 "Estonia 200") and the [Social Democratic Party](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_(Estonia) "Social Democratic Party (Estonia)"), while the [Conservative People's Party](/wiki/Conservative_People%27s_Party_of_Estonia "Conservative People's Party of Estonia"), [Centre Party](/wiki/Estonian_Centre_Party "Estonian Centre Party") and [Isamaa](/wiki/Isamaa "Isamaa") became the opposition.[[194]](#cite_note-198)[[195]](#cite_note-199) In 2024, [Kristen Michal](/wiki/Kristen_Michal "Kristen Michal") became the prime minister.[[196]](#cite_note-200)

### Administrative divisions

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Estonian_administrative_divisions_2017_with_labels.svg/500px-Estonian_administrative_divisions_2017_with_labels.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Estonian_administrative_divisions_2017_with_labels.svg)

Municipalities (local governments) after the 2017 administrative reform

Estonia is a unitary country with a single-tier local government system. Local affairs are managed autonomously by local governments. Since administrative reform in 2017, there are in total 79 local governments, including 15 towns and 64 rural municipalities. All municipalities have equal legal status and form part of a *[maakond](/wiki/Counties_of_Estonia "Counties of Estonia")* (county), which is an [administrative subunit](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Estonia "Administrative divisions of Estonia") of the state.[[197]](#cite_note-201) Representative body of local authorities is municipal council, elected at general direct elections for a four-year term. Each municipal council appoints the local government.[[198]](#cite_note-202) For further decentralization, local governments may form rural municipalities or city districts with limited authority.[[199]](#cite_note-203)

### Law

The [Constitution of Estonia](/wiki/Constitution_of_Estonia "Constitution of Estonia") is the fundamental [law](/wiki/Law_of_Estonia "Law of Estonia"). It is based on five main principles: human dignity, democracy, rule of law, social state, and the Estonian identity.[[200]](#cite_note-204) Estonia has a [civil law](/wiki/Civil_law_(legal_system) "Civil law (legal system)") legal system based on the Germanic legal model.[[201]](#cite_note-205) The court system has a three-level structure. The first instance are county courts which handle all criminal and civil cases, and administrative courts which hear complaints about government and local officials, and other public disputes. The [second instance](/wiki/Appellate_court "Appellate court") are district courts which handle appeals about the first instance decisions.[[202]](#cite_note-206) The [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Estonia "Supreme Court of Estonia") is the [court of cassation](/wiki/Court_of_cassation "Court of cassation"), and conducts constitutional review.[[203]](#cite_note-207) The judiciary is independent, judges are appointed for life, and can be removed from office only when convicted of a crime.[[204]](#cite_note-208) The justice system has been rated among the most efficient in the EU by the EU Justice Scoreboard.[[205]](#cite_note-209)

Law enforcement is managed by agencies under the [Ministry of the Interior](/wiki/Ministry_of_the_Interior_(Estonia) "Ministry of the Interior (Estonia)"). The main agency, the [Police and Border Guard Board](/wiki/Police_and_Border_Guard_Board "Police and Border Guard Board"), oversees protection of public order, investigation of criminal activities, and guarding state border, while the [Estonian Internal Security Service](/wiki/Estonian_Internal_Security_Service "Estonian Internal Security Service") deals with defence of the constitutional order, protection of national secrets, counterintelligence, counterterrorism, and anti-corruption.[[206]](#cite_note-210)[[207]](#cite_note-211)

### Foreign relations

Estonia was a former member of the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations") from 1921 and joined the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") and [OSCE](/wiki/OSCE "OSCE") in 1991.[[208]](#cite_note-212)[[209]](#cite_note-DFAT-213) Estonia joined NATO and the EU in 2004.[[209]](#cite_note-DFAT-213) In 2007, Estonia joined the [Schengen Area](/wiki/Schengen_Area "Schengen Area"), and in 2011 the [Eurozone](/wiki/Eurozone "Eurozone").[[209]](#cite_note-DFAT-213) Tallinn hosts the [eu-LISA](/wiki/Eu-Lisa "Eu-Lisa") systems, operational since 2012,[[210]](#cite_note-214) and Estonia held the rotating [Presidency of the Council of the European Union](/wiki/Presidency_of_the_Council_of_the_European_Union "Presidency of the Council of the European Union") in the second half of 2017.[[211]](#cite_note-215) Estonia is also a member of the [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD"), [WTO](/wiki/WTO "WTO"), and [IMF](/wiki/IMF "IMF").[[209]](#cite_note-DFAT-213)[[212]](#cite_note-216)[[213]](#cite_note-217)

Estonia has engaged in ever closer regional cooperation with [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia "Latvia") and [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania"), and participates in several regional councils, such as the [Baltic Assembly](/wiki/Baltic_Assembly "Baltic Assembly"), the [Baltic Council of Ministers](/wiki/Baltic_Council_of_Ministers "Baltic Council of Ministers"), the [Council of the Baltic Sea States](/wiki/Council_of_the_Baltic_Sea_States "Council of the Baltic Sea States"),[[214]](#cite_note-218) and the [Three Seas Initiative](/wiki/Three_Seas_Initiative "Three Seas Initiative").[[215]](#cite_note-kunnas-219)

Since the end of the Soviet occupation in 1991, [Estonia–Russia relations](/wiki/Estonia%E2%80%93Russia_relations "Estonia–Russia relations") have remained strained.[[216]](#cite_note-220) Relations deteriorated further after [Russia's invasion](/wiki/Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine_(2022%E2%80%93present) "Russian invasion of Ukraine (2022–present)") of [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine") on 24 February 2022; Estonia has strongly supported Ukraine.[[217]](#cite_note-221)[[218]](#cite_note-222)

Estonia has built close relationship with the Nordic countries and is a member of [Nordic-Baltic Eight](/wiki/Nordic-Baltic_Eight "Nordic-Baltic Eight").[[209]](#cite_note-DFAT-213)[[219]](#cite_note-NB8-223) Joint Nordic-Baltic projects include the education programme Nordplus[[220]](#cite_note-224) and mobility programmes for business and industry[[221]](#cite_note-225) and for public administration.[[222]](#cite_note-226) The [Nordic Council of Ministers](/wiki/Nordic_Council_of_Ministers "Nordic Council of Ministers") has an office in Tallinn with a subsidiaries in Tartu and Narva.[[223]](#cite_note-227)[[224]](#cite_note-228) The Baltic states are members of [Nordic Investment Bank](/wiki/Nordic_Investment_Bank "Nordic Investment Bank"), the EU's [Nordic Battle Group](/wiki/Nordic_Battle_Group "Nordic Battle Group"), and in 2011 were invited to co-operate with [Nordic Defence Cooperation](/wiki/Nordic_Defence_Cooperation "Nordic Defence Cooperation") in selected activities.[[225]](#cite_note-229)[[226]](#cite_note-230)[[227]](#cite_note-231)[[228]](#cite_note-232)

### Military

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Baltic_Battalion_Soldiers%2C_Trident_Juncture_15_%2822200204329%29.jpg/250px-Baltic_Battalion_Soldiers%2C_Trident_Juncture_15_%2822200204329%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Baltic_Battalion_Soldiers,_Trident_Juncture_15_(22200204329).jpg)

Estonian soldiers during a NATO exercise in 2015

The [Estonian Defence Forces](/wiki/Estonian_Defence_Forces "Estonian Defence Forces") consist of [land forces](/wiki/Estonian_Land_Forces "Estonian Land Forces"), [navy](/wiki/Estonian_Navy "Estonian Navy"), and [air force](/wiki/Estonian_Air_Force "Estonian Air Force"). The current national military service is compulsory for healthy men between ages of 18 and 28, with conscripts serving 8- or 11-month tours of duty, depending on their education and position provided by the Defence Forces.[[229]](#cite_note-233) The peacetime size of the Estonian Defence Forces is about 6,000 persons, with half of those being conscripts. The planned wartime size of the Defence Forces is 60,000 personnel, including 21,000 personnel in high readiness reserve.[[230]](#cite_note-234) Since 2015, the Estonian defence budget has been over 2% of GDP, fulfilling its NATO defence spending obligation.[[231]](#cite_note-235)

The [Estonian Defence League](/wiki/Estonian_Defence_League "Estonian Defence League") is a voluntary national defence organisation under management of the Ministry of Defence. It is organised based on military principles, has its own military equipment, and provides various military training for its members, including in [guerilla tactics](/wiki/Guerrilla_warfare "Guerrilla warfare"). The Defence League has 18,000 members, with an additional 11,000 volunteers in its affiliated organisations.[[232]](#cite_note-236)[[233]](#cite_note-237)

Estonia co-operates with Latvia and Lithuania in several trilateral defence co-operation initiatives. As part of the [Baltic Air Surveillance Network (BALTNET)](/wiki/Baltic_Air_Surveillance_Network "Baltic Air Surveillance Network") the three countries manage the common airspace control centre, Baltic Battalion (BALTBAT) has participated in the [NATO Response Force](/wiki/NATO_Response_Force "NATO Response Force"), and a joint military educational institution [Baltic Defence College](/wiki/Baltic_Defence_College "Baltic Defence College") is located in Tartu.[[234]](#cite_note-238) Estonia joined [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") on 29 March 2004.[[235]](#cite_note-239) NATO [Cooperative Cyber Defence Centre of Excellence](/wiki/Cooperative_Cyber_Defence_Centre_of_Excellence "Cooperative Cyber Defence Centre of Excellence") was established in Tallinn in 2008.[[236]](#cite_note-240) In response to the [Russian war in Ukraine](/wiki/Russian_military_intervention_in_Ukraine_(2014%E2%80%93present) "Russian military intervention in Ukraine (2014–present)"), since 2017 a [NATO Enhanced Forward Presence](/wiki/NATO_Enhanced_Forward_Presence "NATO Enhanced Forward Presence") battalion battle group has been based in [Tapa Army Base](/wiki/Tapa_Army_Base "Tapa Army Base").[[237]](#cite_note-241) Also part of NATO, the [Baltic Air Policing](/wiki/Baltic_Air_Policing "Baltic Air Policing") deployment has been based in [Ämari Air Base](/wiki/%C3%84mari_Air_Base "Ämari Air Base") since 2014.[[238]](#cite_note-242) In the EU, Estonia participates in [Nordic Battlegroup](/wiki/Nordic_Battlegroup "Nordic Battlegroup") and [Permanent Structured Cooperation](/wiki/Permanent_Structured_Cooperation "Permanent Structured Cooperation").[[239]](#cite_note-243)[[240]](#cite_note-244)

Since 1995, Estonia has participated in numerous international security and peacekeeping missions, including: [Afghanistan](/wiki/International_Security_Assistance_Force "International Security Assistance Force"), [Iraq](/wiki/Multi-National_Force_%E2%80%93_Iraq "Multi-National Force – Iraq"), [Lebanon](/wiki/United_Nations_Interim_Force_in_Lebanon "United Nations Interim Force in Lebanon"), [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo_Force "Kosovo Force"), and [Mali](/wiki/Operation_Barkhane "Operation Barkhane").[[241]](#cite_note-245) Eleven Estonian soldiers have been killed in missions of Afghanistan and Iraq.[[242]](#cite_note-246)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Estonia](/wiki/Economy_of_Estonia "Economy of Estonia")

Estonia is a [developed country](/wiki/Developed_country "Developed country") with an advanced, [high-income economy](/wiki/High-income_economy "High-income economy") that was among the fastest-growing in the EU since its entry in 2004.[[243]](#cite_note-247) With a [GDP (PPP) per capita](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity") of $46,385 in 2023, ranked 40th globally by the [IMF](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund"),[[244]](#cite_note-IMFWEO.EE-248) Estonia ranks highly in international rankings for [education](/wiki/Education_Index "Education Index"),[[245]](#cite_note-249)[[246]](#cite_note-250) [press freedom](/wiki/Press_Freedom_Index "Press Freedom Index"),[[247]](#cite_note-251) [digitalisation of public services](/wiki/E-government "E-government"),[[248]](#cite_note-252)[[249]](#cite_note-253) the prevalence of technology companies,[[250]](#cite_note-254) and maintains very high rankings in the [Human Development Index](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_Human_Development_Index "List of countries by Human Development Index").[[251]](#cite_note-HDI-255) Free education[[252]](#cite_note-256) and the longest paid [maternity leave](/wiki/Maternity_leave "Maternity leave") in the OECD[[253]](#cite_note-257) are also distinctive characteristics of modern Estonian social fabric.

Telecommunications, energy, textiles, chemical products, banking, services, food and fishery, timber, shipbuilding, electronics, and transportation are key sectors of the economy.[[254]](#cite_note-258) Historically, the locally mined [oil shale](/wiki/Oil_shale "Oil shale") was the main source of energy, contributing over 85% of energy production in the early 2010s,[[255]](#cite_note-259) with renewable sources like wood, peat, and biomass accounting for the remaining part of primary energy production. The share of wind energy, comprising only 6% of energy consumption in 2009,[[256]](#cite_note-260) has been rapidly growing in recent years.

The [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis") impacted Estonia with an initial contraction of GDP, which led to governmental budget adjustments to stabilise the economy. By 2010, the economy began to recover driven by exports, and annual industrial output increased by over 20%.[[257]](#cite_note-Stat-261) Real GDP growth in 2011 reached 8%, and in 2012, Estonia was the only eurozone country with a budget surplus, with national debt at 6%, among the lowest in EU. Despite economic disparities between regions – over half of the GDP is generated in the capital city Tallinn – the country has continued to perform well, including a notable first-place ranking in the [Environmental Performance Index](/wiki/Environmental_Performance_Index "Environmental Performance Index") in 2024.[[258]](#cite_note-262)

### Public policy

Estonia's economy continues to benefit from a transparent government and policies that sustain a high level of [economic freedom](/wiki/Index_of_Economic_Freedom "Index of Economic Freedom"), ranking 6th globally and 2nd in Europe.[[259]](#cite_note-263)[[260]](#cite_note-264) The rule of [law](/wiki/Law_of_Estonia "Law of Estonia") remains strongly buttressed and enforced by an independent and efficient judicial system. A simplified tax system with flat rates and low indirect taxation, openness to foreign investment, and a liberal trade regime have supported the resilient and well-functioning economy.[[261]](#cite_note-taxfoundation1-265) As of May 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Estonia&action=edit), the [Ease of Doing Business Index](/wiki/Ease_of_Doing_Business_Index "Ease of Doing Business Index") by the [World Bank Group](/wiki/World_Bank_Group "World Bank Group") places the country 16th in the world.[[262]](#cite_note-266) The strong focus on the IT sector through its [e-Estonia](/wiki/E-Estonia "E-Estonia") programme has led to much faster, simpler and efficient public services where for example filing a tax return takes less than five minutes and 98% of banking transactions are conducted through the internet.[[263]](#cite_note-267)[[264]](#cite_note-268) Estonia has the 13th lowest business bribery risk in the world, according to TRACE Matrix.[[265]](#cite_note-traceinternational.org-269)

After restoring independence, in the 1990s, Estonia eagerly pursued economic reform and reintegration with other Western democracies.[[266]](#cite_note-270) In 1994, applying the economic theories of [Milton Friedman](/wiki/Milton_Friedman "Milton Friedman"), Estonia became one of the first countries to adopt a [flat tax](/wiki/Flat_tax "Flat tax"), with a uniform rate of 26% regardless of personal income. This rate has since been reduced several times, e.g., to 24% in 2005, 23% in 2006, and to 21% in 2008.[[267]](#cite_note-271) The [Government of Estonia](/wiki/Government_of_Estonia "Government of Estonia") adopted the euro on 1 January 2011.[[268]](#cite_note-euro-EFP-272)

### Transport

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Tallinn_asv2022-04_img16_Vanasadam_Tallink.jpg/250px-Tallinn_asv2022-04_img16_Vanasadam_Tallink.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tallinn_asv2022-04_img16_Vanasadam_Tallink.jpg)

[Cruiseferries](/wiki/Cruiseferry "Cruiseferry") connect Estonia to neighbouring Finland and Sweden (Tallinn, 2022).

The primary modes of [Transport in Estonia](/wiki/Transport_in_Estonia "Transport in Estonia") include road, rail, maritime, and air transport, each contributing significantly to the economy and accessibility of the region. [Port of Tallinn](/wiki/Port_of_Tallinn "Port of Tallinn") is one of the largest maritime enterprises in the Baltic Sea, catering to both cargo and passenger traffic.
