Among the facilities is the [ice-free port](/wiki/Ice-free_port "Ice-free port") of [Muuga](/wiki/Port_of_Muuga "Port of Muuga"), located near Tallinn, which boasts modern transhipment capabilities, a high-capacity grain elevator, chill and frozen storage, and enhanced oil tanker offloading facilities.[[269]](#cite_note-273) Estonian shipping company [Tallink](/wiki/Tallink "Tallink") operates a fleet of [Baltic Sea cruiseferries](/wiki/Baltic_Sea_cruiseferries "Baltic Sea cruiseferries") and [ropax](/wiki/Ropax "Ropax") ships. Tallink is the largest passenger and cargo shipping operator in the Baltic Sea, with routes connecting Estonia to Finland and Sweden. The ferry lines to Estonian islands are operated by *[TS Laevad](/wiki/TS_Laevad "TS Laevad")* and *[Kihnu Veeteed](/wiki/Kihnu_Veeteed "Kihnu Veeteed")*.[[270]](#cite_note-274)

[Estonia's railway network](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Estonia "Rail transport in Estonia"), primarily operated by the state-owned [Eesti Raudtee](/wiki/Eesti_Raudtee "Eesti Raudtee"), encompasses over 2,000 km, including the 209.6 km (130.2 mi) Tallinn–Narva line, which also serves as a link to [St. Petersburg](/wiki/St._Petersburg "St. Petersburg").[[271]](#cite_note-275) Most of Estonia's original [narrow-gauge railways](/wiki/Narrow-gauge_railways_in_Estonia "Narrow-gauge railways in Estonia") were dismantled during the Soviet occupation, however, the narrow-gauge [tram lines in Tallinn](/wiki/Trams_in_Tallinn "Trams in Tallinn") are in service to this day. The country now primarily operates on [5 ft and 1520 mm gauge railways](/wiki/5_ft_and_1520_mm_gauge_railways "5 ft and 1520 mm gauge railways"). Since 2017, a major infrastructure project [Rail Baltica](/wiki/Rail_Baltica "Rail Baltica") has been in process in order to integrate Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania to the European [standard gauge](/wiki/Standard_gauge "Standard gauge") railway system.[[272]](#cite_note-276)

[Highways in Estonia](/wiki/Highways_in_Estonia "Highways in Estonia") are extensive, with 16,982 km of state-managed roads, including 12,716 km of paved surfaces, ensuring reliable transport across the country.[[273]](#cite_note-277) Major motorways such as the [Road 1](/wiki/Narva_Highway "Narva Highway") ([E20](/wiki/European_route_E20 "European route E20")), [Road 2](/wiki/Tartu_Highway "Tartu Highway") ([E263](/wiki/European_route_E263 "European route E263")), and [Road 4](/wiki/P%C3%A4rnu_Highway "Pärnu Highway") ([E67](/wiki/European_route_E67 "European route E67")), are essential for both local and international travel. Estonia has a high rate of [car](/wiki/Car "Car") ownership, with most households owning at least one vehicle, and nearly half owning two.[[274]](#cite_note-278)

The [Tallinn Airport](/wiki/Tallinn_Airport "Tallinn Airport") is the largest in Estonia and serves as a secondary hub for [AirBaltic](/wiki/AirBaltic "AirBaltic")[[275]](#cite_note-279) and [LOT Polish Airlines](/wiki/LOT_Polish_Airlines "LOT Polish Airlines").[[276]](#cite_note-280) Other airports with regular passenger flights are located in [Tartu](/wiki/Tartu_Airport "Tartu Airport"), [Pärnu](/wiki/P%C3%A4rnu_Airport "Pärnu Airport"), [Kuressaare](/wiki/Kuressaare_Airport "Kuressaare Airport"), and [Kärdla](/wiki/K%C3%A4rdla_Airport "Kärdla Airport").

### Natural resources and mining

Estonia is relatively rich in natural resources, although many are not found in economically viable quantities or face technical and environmental challenges that hinder their extraction. The country has large [oil shale](/wiki/Oil_shale "Oil shale") and [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone") deposits. In addition, Estonia also has large reserves of [phosphorite](/wiki/Phosphorite "Phosphorite"), [pitchblende](/wiki/Uraninite "Uraninite"), and [granite](/wiki/Granite "Granite") that currently are not mined, or not mined extensively.[[277]](#cite_note-281) The underground resources may include [gold](/wiki/Gold "Gold"), [molybdenum](/wiki/Molybdenum "Molybdenum"), [platinum](/wiki/Platinum "Platinum"), [vanadium](/wiki/Vanadium "Vanadium"), and [strontium](/wiki/Strontium "Strontium"). Future potential resources are thought to include [diatomaceous earth](/wiki/Diatomaceous_earth "Diatomaceous earth") and [uranium](/wiki/Uranium "Uranium"). There are also indications of [oil](/wiki/Oil "Oil") near Hiiumaa and [natural gas](/wiki/Natural_gas "Natural gas") reserves in north Estonia. Currently, the most significant resources being exploited in Estonia are oil shale and phosphorite, along with natural building materials such as [sand](/wiki/Sand "Sand"), [gravel](/wiki/Gravel "Gravel"), limestone, and [clay](/wiki/Clay "Clay").[[278]](#cite_note-282)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/View_of_Kivi%C3%B5li.jpg/250px-View_of_Kivi%C3%B5li.jpg)](/wiki/File:View_of_Kivi%C3%B5li.jpg)

Oil shale region in the northeast of the country

As of 2013, the [oil shale industry in Estonia](/wiki/Oil_shale_in_Estonia "Oil shale in Estonia") was among the most developed globally,[[279]](#cite_note-283) supplying approximately 70% of the country's total primary energy needs and contributing about 4% to the GDP in 2012.[[280]](#cite_note-audit-284)[[281]](#cite_note-285) Additionally, significant quantities of [rare-earth](/wiki/Rare-earth "Rare-earth") oxides are found in the tailings from over 50 years of [uranium ore](/wiki/Uranium_ore "Uranium ore"), [shale](/wiki/Shale "Shale") and [loparite](/wiki/Loparite "Loparite") mining at [Sillamäe](/wiki/Sillam%C3%A4e "Sillamäe").[[282]](#cite_note-286) The rising global prices for rare earth elements have made the extraction of these oxides economically viable, with Estonia currently exporting around 3,000 tonnes annually, accounting for approximately 2% of global production.[[283]](#cite_note-287)

### Energy

The energy sector in Estonia has since the 1920s been dominated by the oil shale industry. However, the production of electricity from oil shale has declined since the 1990s.[[284]](#cite_note-288) The oil shale industry, concentrated in [Ida-Viru County](/wiki/Ida-Viru_County "Ida-Viru County"), produces around 73% of the entire country's electricity.[[285]](#cite_note-289) In recent years, however, there has been a significant growth in renewable energy generation, alongside ongoing discussions regarding the potential future use of nuclear energy. The largest producer of electricity and thermal energy in Estonia is the state-owned company [Eesti Energia](/wiki/Eesti_Energia "Eesti Energia"). Oil shale remains the primary energy source in Estonia, primarily utilised for electricity generation and heating, particularly in Narva. In addition to electricity, Estonia has been increasingly producing oil from this resource, with production volumes steadily rising. Other energy sources include peat, firewood, hydroelectric and wind energy, solar panels, and imported natural and liquefied gas, as well as coal.[[286]](#cite_note-290)

Estonia enjoys one of the lowest dependencies on energy imports within the EU. This is largely due to the high share of domestic energy sources, including oil shale and an increasing proportion of renewable energy, such as biomass, wind, solar power, and improved energy efficiency in production, transmission, and consumption. The diversity of suppliers for electricity, gas, liquid fuels, and solid fuels has contributed to competitive, market-based energy prices for consumers.[[287]](#cite_note-tulemusaruanne-291) In a notable policy shift, Estonia has prohibited the import of pipeline gas from Russia as of 2023. This follows a decade during which Russian gas accounted for 100% of the country's consumption. As of 2023, Estonia's gas consumption was recorded at 3.42 TWh, supported by a strategic gas reserve of 1 TWh located in the [Inčukalns Municipality](/wiki/In%C4%8Dukalns_Municipality "Inčukalns Municipality") underground gas storage facility in Latvia, equating to approximately 29% of the country's average annual gas needs.[[287]](#cite_note-tulemusaruanne-291)

#### Wind power

There has also been increased investment in renewable energy sources,.[[288]](#cite_note-292)[[289]](#cite_note-293)[[290]](#cite_note-294) Wind power is steadily expanding; current production is nearly 60 MW, with an additional 399 MW of projects underway and over 2,800 MW proposed in areas such as [Lake Peipus](/wiki/Lake_Peipus "Lake Peipus") and the coastal regions of [Hiiumaa](/wiki/Hiiumaa "Hiiumaa").

[![Aseriaru wind turbines next to fields](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Elektrituulikud.jpg/250px-Elektrituulikud.jpg)](/wiki/File:Elektrituulikud.jpg)

Wind farm in [Aseriaru](/wiki/Aseriaru "Aseriaru")

The development of offshore wind expanded rapidly in the 2020s. In summer 2024 Norwegian firm Deep Wind Offshore won two auctions (Saare 2.1 and 2.2) to build offshore wind farms totalling over 1GW of generating capacity.[[291]](#cite_note-295) Estonian and French renewables developers Sunly and Valorem joined the DWO consortia, now named *Tuul Energy*.[[292]](#cite_note-296)

In January 2025 French offshore wind developer Oxan Energy, founded by former staff of [Shell](/wiki/Shell_plc "Shell plc"), won the auction for build a 900 floating offshore wind farm 60 km west of [Saaremaa](/wiki/Saaremaa "Saaremaa") island.[[293]](#cite_note-297)

#### Nuclear power

While Estonia, along with Lithuania, Poland, and Latvia, considered participating in the construction of the [Visaginas nuclear power plant](/wiki/Visaginas_nuclear_power_plant "Visaginas nuclear power plant") in Lithuania,[[294]](#cite_note-wnn-lt-298)[[295]](#cite_note-le-299) the project faced delays and challenges, prompting Eesti Energia to shift its focus to shale oil production, viewed as more profitable.[[296]](#cite_note-300)

#### Power market

The Estonian [electricity market](/wiki/Electricity_market "Electricity market") was [liberalised](/wiki/Liberalization "Liberalization") in 2013,[[297]](#cite_note-301) integrating into the [Nord Pool Spot](/wiki/Nord_Pool_Spot "Nord Pool Spot") network.[[298]](#cite_note-302)

### Agriculture, fishery and forestry

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/View_to_Aruk%C3%BCla.JPG/250px-View_to_Aruk%C3%BCla.JPG)](/wiki/File:View_to_Aruk%C3%BCla.JPG)

Crops near Aruküla in Northern Estonia

Historically, Estonia has been the northernmost country that can grow grain for export.[[299]](#cite_note-303)
In the 1990s, agricultural significance in the economy declined sharply as large Soviet-era collective farms were dismantled and privatised. In recent years, large enterprises have once again become dominant, while smaller farms focus on niche markets, organic farming, and rural tourism. Recent years have seen an increase in Estonia's cultivated land, with approximately 1.05 million hectares of arable land and 0.24 million hectares of natural grasslands recorded by 2019.[[300]](#cite_note-304)[[301]](#cite_note-305) Estonia has one of the largest average farm sizes within the EU at 62 hectares per farm, with around 78% of farmland owned by entities managing at least 100 hectares – far above the EU average of 49% for this ownership category.[[302]](#cite_note-306) Estonia ranks second in Europe, following [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria"), in the proportion of farmland under [organic cultivation](/wiki/Organic_farming "Organic farming").[[303]](#cite_note-307)

About 95% of the fish caught in Estonia's waters comes from the Baltic Sea, with the remainder sourced from inland waters. Distance fishing is also practiced. The Baltic Sea fisheries distinguish between migratory species, such as sprat, herring, cod, and salmon, which are subject to EU quotas, and local species, such as perch and pike, which are managed domestically. Despite stringent regulations, Estonian fish stocks face significant challenges, including habitat loss, overfishing, and decreased spawning areas. In response, Estonia has introduced conservation initiatives to protect vulnerable fish species and enhance breeding programmes.[[304]](#cite_note-308)

The forestry sector is a significant contributor to Estonian economy. The Estonian government aims to establish sustainable annual harvest levels that align with EU climate objectives, while the industry pushes for higher harvest quotas to ensure profitability and job security. Conversely, conservationists advocate for reduced logging to safeguard biodiversity and fulfill climate commitments. Currently, the government maintains a harvest threshold of at least 9.5 million m3 to balance economic impacts and environmental goals. However, inventories indicate serious over-harvesting, leading to intensified debates about how to sustain the timber industry while protecting Estonia's rich biodiversity.[[305]](#cite_note-309) Since at least 2009, logging has increased significantly across both private and protected lands, including national parks.[[306]](#cite_note-310) While Estonia's logging practices need to be reduced to enhance biodiversity and achieve carbon sequestration goals, the sector continues to expand; in 2022, the state forestry agency [RMK](/wiki/State_Forest_Management_Centre "State Forest Management Centre") reported a record profit of 1.4 billion euros.[[307]](#cite_note-311)

### Industry and services

Industry is the backbone of Estonia's economy. The manufacturing sector is the largest segment of industry, accounting for over 15% of the GDP. This sector provides employment for around one-fifth of the workforce, equating to about 120,000 individuals. Additionally, the export turnover of industrial enterprises typically represents around two-thirds of Estonia's total export volume.[[308]](#cite_note-312) Food, construction, and electronic industries are currently among the most important branches of Estonia's industry.[[309]](#cite_note-313) Key branches within Estonia's industry include food production, construction, and electronics, with the construction industry alone employing over 80,000 people in 2007, roughly 12% of the total workforce.[[310]](#cite_note-314) The machinery and chemical industries are also significant, primarily concentrated in [Ida-Viru County](/wiki/Ida-Viru_County "Ida-Viru County") and around [Tallinn](/wiki/Tallinn "Tallinn").

The Estonian manufacturing sector comprises 7,981 enterprises, representing 8% of all businesses in the country. This sector employs over 107,000 individuals, accounting for 22% of all employed persons in Estonia. In 2020, the total profit margin for manufacturing companies was 4.9%. A significant portion of the sector consists of micro-enterprises, with 78% of manufacturing firms employing fewer than 10 workers, while only 3% of companies have more than 100 employees (totaling 215 firms). Approximately 17% of manufacturing enterprises report sales revenues exceeding 1 million euros, and exports contribute to 52% of the sector's total sales revenue.[[311]](#cite_note-315) The most significant branch of the manufacturing sector is machinery production, which accounts for approximately 25% of total output. Other key industries include wood and paper production (20%), food processing (15%), chemical production (10%), metalworking (13%), and light industry, which constitutes less than 5% of the total output. In 2018, Estonia's exported goods amounted to €10.4 billion, representing 72% of the country's total merchandise exports. The manufacturing sector employed around 124,000 individuals and contributed 15.4% to Estonia's GDP, with 20% of the GDP growth that year stemming from this sector.[[312]](#cite_note-316)[[313]](#cite_note-317)

In terms of value added, the manufacturing sector's share of the Estonian economy is slightly below the EU average (at 15%). However, Estonia has one of the highest proportions of employment in manufacturing among EU countries, with nearly one-fifth of the workforce engaged in this sector. Manufacturing is the largest employer in Estonia, with significant job creation occurring in 2019, particularly in the production of electrical equipment and the repair and installation of machinery and equipment. The wood industry saw the highest growth in production volume during that year. The major industrial sectors by employment are wood processing, food production, and metalworking. The sector is heavily reliant on external markets, with over 60% of its output being exported. Key export markets include Finland and Sweden, which also account for more than 60% of foreign direct investments in Estonia's manufacturing industry.[[314]](#cite_note-318)

In the 2000s, there was a notable shift in Estonia's economic structure, with the services sector's contribution to GDP increasing while agriculture and industry saw a decline in their share. Currently, services account for 68.1% of Estonia's GDP and employ 76.8% of the workforce. Despite its growth, certain service-related sectors often offer some of the lowest wages in the economy.[[315]](#cite_note-319)

### Science and technology

See also: [Space science in Estonia](/wiki/Space_science_in_Estonia "Space science in Estonia")

Estonia is a member of the international scientific organisations [CERN](/wiki/CERN "CERN"),[[316]](#cite_note-320) [ESA](/wiki/ESA "ESA"),[[317]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-321) [Euratom](/wiki/Euratom "Euratom") and [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO"). The [Estonian Academy of Sciences](/wiki/Estonian_Academy_of_Sciences "Estonian Academy of Sciences") is the [national academy](/wiki/National_academy "National academy") of science. The strongest public non-profit research institute that carries out fundamental and applied research is the [National Institute of Chemical Physics and Biophysics](/wiki/National_Institute_of_Chemical_Physics_and_Biophysics "National Institute of Chemical Physics and Biophysics"). As of 2015[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Estonia&action=edit), Estonia spends around 1.5% of its GDP on [Research and Development](/wiki/Research_and_Development "Research and Development"), compared to an EU average of around 2.0%.[[318]](#cite_note-322) Estonia has been ranked 16th in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2025.[[319]](#cite_note-323)[[320]](#cite_note-324)

[![ESTCube-1 micro satellite orbiting globe and beaming light to Estonia](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/ESTCube_orbiidil_2.jpg/250px-ESTCube_orbiidil_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:ESTCube_orbiidil_2.jpg)

[ESTCube-1](/wiki/ESTCube-1 "ESTCube-1") was the first Estonian satellite.

Estonia has established a strong information technology sector, a development partly attributed to the *[Tiigrihüpe](/wiki/Tiigrih%C3%BCpe "Tiigrihüpe")* project initiated in the mid-1990s. The country is often cited as one of the most "wired" and advanced in Europe concerning e-government initiatives.[[321]](#cite_note-325) The [e-residency programme](/wiki/E-residency_of_Estonia "E-residency of Estonia"), launched in 2014, extended various digital services to non-residents. Notable tech startups include [Skype](/wiki/Skype "Skype"),[[322]](#cite_note-326) [Bolt](/wiki/Bolt_(company) "Bolt (company)"), [GrabCAD](/wiki/GrabCAD "GrabCAD"), [Fortumo](/wiki/Fortumo "Fortumo") and [Wise](/wiki/TransferWise "TransferWise"). The country reportedly holds the highest startup-per-person ratio globally, with 1,291 startups as of January 2022, including seven [unicorn companies](/wiki/Unicorn_(finance) "Unicorn (finance)"), translating to nearly one startup for every 1,000 Estonians.[[323]](#cite_note-327)[[324]](#cite_note-328)[[325]](#cite_note-329)

[Space science in Estonia](/wiki/Space_science_in_Estonia "Space science in Estonia") is anchored by the [Tartu Observatory](/wiki/Tartu_Observatory "Tartu Observatory"). Since the 2000s, Estonia has re-engaged with the space sector, signing a cooperation treaty with the [European Space Agency](/wiki/European_Space_Agency "European Space Agency") in 2007 and officially joining in 2015, leading to collaborative projects such as the [Gaia mission](/wiki/Gaia_mission "Gaia mission") and the successful launches of research satellites [ESTCube-1](/wiki/ESTCube-1 "ESTCube-1") in 2013 and [ESTCube-2](/wiki/ESTCube-2 "ESTCube-2") in 2023.

Estonia is the first state to sponsor personal [genetic testing](/wiki/Genetic_testing "Genetic testing") services in order to minimise, and prevent, genetically caused health problems, e.g., adult-onset diabetes and cardiovascular diseases. The government also plans to provide lifestyle advice in a pilot project based on the [DNA](/wiki/DNA "DNA") data of 100,000 Estonians.[[326]](#cite_note-330)

## Demographics

[![The population of Estonia, from 1960 to 2019, with a peak in 1990.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Eesti_rahvaarv_1960-2019.png/330px-Eesti_rahvaarv_1960-2019.png)](/wiki/File:Eesti_rahvaarv_1960-2019.png)

Population of Estonia 1960–2019. The changes are largely attributed to immigration from, and emigration to, Russia and other parts of the former USSR.[[327]](#cite_note-stat.ee_2-331)

Major changes in the [demographics of Estonia](/wiki/Demographics_of_Estonia "Demographics of Estonia") have taken place after the country restored independence in 1991.[[328]](#cite_note-332) Some of the more notable changes have taken effect in the level of stratification and distribution of family income. The [Gini coefficient](/wiki/Gini_coefficient "Gini coefficient") has held steadily higher than the EU average (31 in 2009),[[329]](#cite_note-333) although it has dropped. The registered unemployment rate in 2024 was 7.6%.[[330]](#cite_note-334)

Estonia's population on 1 January 2025 (1,369,285 people) was about 3% higher than in the previous census of 2021. 211 different self-reported ethnic groups are represented in the country's population and 243 different mother tongues are spoken. Census data indicate that Estonia has continued to stand out among European countries for its highly educated population – 43% of the population aged 25–64 have a university education, which puts Estonia in 7th place in Europe (Estonian women rank 3rd).[[331]](#cite_note-PopulationByNationality-335)

More people of different ethnic origin live in Estonia than ever before, but the share of Estonians in the population has remained stable over the three censuses (2000: 68.3%; 2011: 69.8%; 2021: 69.4%).[[332]](#cite_note-336)[[331]](#cite_note-PopulationByNationality-335) Estonia is generally ethnically homogeneous, with 13 of its 15 counties having over 80% ethnic Estonian populations; the most homogeneous county is [Hiiumaa](/wiki/Hiiumaa "Hiiumaa"), where 98.4% of residents are ethnic Estonians. However, in [Harju County](/wiki/Harju_County "Harju County"), which includes the capital [Tallinn](/wiki/Tallinn "Tallinn"), and [Ida-Viru County](/wiki/Ida-Viru_County "Ida-Viru County"), the demographic makeup is more diverse due to a significant Russian-speaking minority. Ethnic Estonians make up around 60% of the population in Harju County and only about 20% in Ida-Viru, where the Russian-speaking community forms nearly 70% of residents. This [ethnic Russian](/wiki/History_of_Russians_in_Estonia "History of Russians in Estonia") minority comprises about 24% of Estonia's total population, largely a result of Soviet-era immigration, and exists alongside recent Ukrainian refugees who arrived in 2022, now representing around 6% of the national population.[[333]](#cite_note-337)

Historically, Estonia, as well as Latvia, had a significant [Baltic Germans](/wiki/Baltic_German "Baltic German") community. In the 12th and 13th centuries, [Catholic German](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Germany "Catholic Church in Germany") traders and crusaders (*see [Ostsiedlung](/wiki/Ostsiedlung "Ostsiedlung")*) began settling in the eastern Baltic territories.[[334]](#cite_note-338) The [Baltic German nobility](/wiki/Baltic_German_nobility "Baltic German nobility") significantly influenced Estonian culture. German language maintained its leading position until the Russification policy of the tsarist era at the end of the 19th century, and sometimes even longer. The German-speaking population mostly left Estonia in 1939.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Ruhnu_puukirik.jpg/250px-Ruhnu_puukirik.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ruhnu_puukirik.jpg)

The [church](/wiki/Saint_Magdalene_Church,_Ruhnu "Saint Magdalene Church, Ruhnu") (built in 1644) in the [Swedish](/wiki/Estonian_Swedes "Estonian Swedes") village of [Ruhnu](/wiki/Ruhnu "Ruhnu"), now the oldest preserved wooden shrine in Estonia

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Saare_k%C3%BCla_Piirissaar.JPG/250px-Saare_k%C3%BCla_Piirissaar.JPG)](/wiki/File:Saare_k%C3%BCla_Piirissaar.JPG)

A [Russian](/wiki/Russians_in_Estonia "Russians in Estonia") [Old Believer](/wiki/Old_Believer "Old Believer") village with an Orthodox church in [Piirissaar](/wiki/Piirissaar "Piirissaar"), an island of [Lake Peipus](/wiki/Lake_Peipus "Lake Peipus")

Historically, large parts of Estonia's northwestern coast and islands have been populated by the indigenous ethnic group of [Estonian Swedes](/wiki/Estonian_Swedes "Estonian Swedes"). In recent years, the number of Swedish residents in Estonia has risen again, numbering almost 500 people by 2008, owing to property reforms enacted in the early 1990s. In 2004, the [Ingrian Finnish](/wiki/Ingrian_Finns "Ingrian Finns") minority in Estonia elected a cultural council and was granted cultural autonomy. The Estonian Swedes minority similarly received cultural autonomy in 2007.[[335]](#cite_note-National_Minorities_Cultural_Autonomy_Act_(2019_version)-339) There is also a [Roma](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people") community of approximately 1,000–1,500.[[336]](#cite_note-340)

As of 2 July 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Estonia&action=edit), 84.1% of Estonian residents were citizens of Estonia, 8.6% were citizens of other countries and 7.3% were "citizens with undetermined citizenship".[[337]](#cite_note-341) Estonia has also accepted [quota refugees](/wiki/European_migrant_crisis#Relocation_and_resettlement_of_asylum_seekers "European migrant crisis") under the migrant plan agreed upon by EU member states in 2015.[[338]](#cite_note-342) The 2008 [United Nations Human Rights Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Human_Rights_Council "United Nations Human Rights Council") report called "extremely credible" the description of the [citizenship policy of Estonia](/wiki/Estonian_nationality_law "Estonian nationality law") as "discriminatory".[[339]](#cite_note-343) Estonian Russians have developed their own identity – more than half of the respondents recognised that Estonian Russians differ noticeably from the Russians in Russia.[[340]](#cite_note-Societal_survey-344)
The Estonian Cultural Autonomy law that was passed in 1925 was unique in Europe at that time.[[341]](#cite_note-345) Cultural autonomies could be granted to [minorities](/wiki/Minorities "Minorities") numbering more than 3,000 people with longstanding ties to the Republic of Estonia. The Law on Cultural Autonomy for National Minorities was reinstated in 1993.

### Urbanization

Further information: [List of cities and towns in Estonia](/wiki/List_of_cities_and_towns_in_Estonia "List of cities and towns in Estonia") and [Populated places in Estonia](/wiki/Populated_places_in_Estonia "Populated places in Estonia")

According to the Estonian government's regulations established in 2004, populated areas in Estonia are categorised as follows: *küla* (village), *alevik* (small borough or rarely small town), *alev* (town), and *linn* (city). A *küla* is typically a sparsely populated area or a densely populated settlement with fewer than 300 permanent residents. An *alevik* generally has at least 300 permanent residents, while both *alev* and *linn* are classified as densely populated areas with at least 1,000 residents. As of 2024, Estonia has 47 cities, 13 towns, 186 small boroughs, and 4,457 villages, with Tallinn being the capital and largest city, located on the northern coast along the [Gulf of Finland](/wiki/Gulf_of_Finland "Gulf of Finland").[[342]](#cite_note-346)

In the 21st century, Estonia has continued to experience urbanisation. More than 70% of Estonia's population now reside in cities and towns.[[343]](#cite_note-347) The population density in Estonia averages around 30.6 people per square km, with significant regional variations. The lowest density is found on [Hiiumaa](/wiki/Hiiumaa "Hiiumaa") at 10.2 people per square km, while [Harju County](/wiki/Harju_County "Harju County"), which includes Tallinn, has the highest density at 121.3 people per square km.[[344]](#cite_note-348)

### Religion

Main article: [Religion in Estonia](/wiki/Religion_in_Estonia "Religion in Estonia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/R%C3%B5uge_Maarja_kirik_2023_04.jpg/250px-R%C3%B5uge_Maarja_kirik_2023_04.jpg)](/wiki/File:R%C3%B5uge_Maarja_kirik_2023_04.jpg)

A Lutheran parish church in Rõuge

Estonia's constitution guarantees freedom of religion, separation of church and state, and individual rights to privacy of belief and religion.[[346]](#cite_note-350) Estonia is one of the [least religious](/wiki/Irreligion_by_country "Irreligion by country") countries in the world, with majority of the population claiming to be [irreligious](/wiki/Irreligion "Irreligion").[[347]](#cite_note-351)[[348]](#cite_note-gallup-religiosity-poor-nations-352)

Traditionally, the largest religious denomination in the country has been the [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") church, to which 86,030 people (or 7.7% of the population) still belong, according to self-reported data of the 2021 census.[[349]](#cite_note-stat2021-353) However, since the 2011 census, Eastern Orthodoxy has surpassed Lutheranism in terms of church membership in Estonia. While not being a [state church](/wiki/State_religion "State religion"), the [Estonian Evangelical Lutheran Church](/wiki/Estonian_Evangelical_Lutheran_Church "Estonian Evangelical Lutheran Church") had historically been the [national church](/wiki/National_church "National church"). An agreement giving preferential status to the Lutheran church ended in 2023.[[350]](#cite_note-354) Before World War II, around 80% of the population of Estonian were Protestants, and the vast majority Lutheran,[[351]](#cite_note-MarshallCavendish2010-355) followed by [Baptists](/wiki/Baptists "Baptists") and other Protestant branches. Religious affiliation in Estonia has decreased substantially over the past century.[[352]](#cite_note-356)

Approximately 29% of Estonia's population identified with a religion according to data collected from the 2021 census. In contrast, 58% of Estonians reported having no religious affiliation. Among those who identify with a religion, 93% consider themselves Christians, a slight decrease from 97% in 2011. Other religions, including Islam, represent smaller but gradually growing segments; for instance, the Muslim population of Estonia grew from 0.1% to 0.5%.[[353]](#cite_note-ligikaudu-357)

A 2015 study by [Pew Research](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center") found that the 45% of respondents who declared themselves to be [religiously unaffiliated](/wiki/Irreligion "Irreligion"), were divided between 9% as atheists, 1% as agnostics, and 35% as believing in "nothing in particular".[[354]](#cite_note-358)

Eastern Orthodoxy is now the largest religious group, primarily observed by a minority within the Russian-speaking minority, as well as the [Setos](/wiki/Setos "Setos"), a small ethnic Estonian group. The two largest Orthodox communities are the [Estonian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Estonian_Orthodox_Church_of_Moscow_Patriarchate "Estonian Orthodox Church of Moscow Patriarchate") of [Moscow Patriarchate](/wiki/Moscow_Patriarchate "Moscow Patriarchate"), and the [Estonian Apostolic Orthodox Church](/wiki/Estonian_Apostolic_Orthodox_Church "Estonian Apostolic Orthodox Church") of the [Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople](/wiki/Ecumenical_Patriarchate_of_Constantinople "Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople"). Since the 17th century, a small community of Russian Orthodox [Old Believers](/wiki/Old_Believers "Old Believers") has resided by the coast of Lake Peipus.

### Languages

Main article: [Languages of Estonia](/wiki/Languages_of_Estonia "Languages of Estonia")

The official language is [Estonian](/wiki/Estonian_language "Estonian language"). Estonian is spoken by 84% of the population: 67% of people speak it as their mother tongue and 17% as a foreign language.

The [South Estonian](/wiki/South_Estonian "South Estonian") dialects, which include [Mulgi](/wiki/Mulgi_dialect "Mulgi dialect"), [Tartu](/wiki/Tartu_dialect "Tartu dialect"), [Võro](/wiki/V%C3%B5ro_dialect "Võro dialect") and [Seto](/wiki/Seto_dialect "Seto dialect"), are spoken by nearly 100,000 people. Although they are [distinct](/wiki/Genetic_relationship_(linguistics) "Genetic relationship (linguistics)") from "standard" (North) Estonian, they are typically regarded as dialects or regional forms of Estonian, rather than separate languages.[[355]](#cite_note-359)
After English, Russian is now the second most widely spoken foreign language in Estonia. Most of the native Russian-speakers are first and second generation immigrants residing in the capital city Tallinn and several other industrial urban areas (most notably in [Narva](/wiki/Narva "Narva"), where majority of the population are ethnic Russians). Due to its dominant role in the recent past (Russian language was a compulsory subject in all schools in Soviet-occupied Estonia), many Estonians, particularly those now aged 50 to 80, can still speak good Russian. By 2010, nearly two-thirds of the native Russian-speakers of Estonia had learnt to speak Estonian proficiently.[[356]](#cite_note-360)

Historically, Swedish-speaking communities lived in Estonia from the 13th century until the 20th century, particularly along the coast and on the islands. After the establishment of Estonian independence, these communities were officially recognised, with Swedish used as an administrative language in majority-Swedish municipalities. However, during World War II, most Swedish speakers fled to Sweden ahead of the Soviet occupation in 1944, leaving only a small number of elderly Swedish speakers in Estonia. Swedish influence is still evident, particularly in regions like [Noarootsi Parish](/wiki/Noarootsi_Parish "Noarootsi Parish") of [Lääne County](/wiki/L%C3%A4%C3%A4ne_County "Lääne County"), where bilingual Estonian-Swedish place names and signs remain.[[357]](#cite_note-361)[[358]](#cite_note-362)

The most common foreign languages learnt by Estonian students are English, Russian, German, and French.[[359]](#cite_note-363) [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") is the most widely spoken foreign language in Estonia today. According to 2021 census data 76% of the population can speak a foreign language. In the 2021 census 17% of the native speakers of standard Estonian reported that they can also speak a dialect of Estonian.[[360]](#cite_note-364)[[331]](#cite_note-PopulationByNationality-335)

[Estonian Sign Language](/wiki/Estonian_Sign_Language "Estonian Sign Language"), officially adopted in 2007 under the Estonian Language Act, is the primary sign language of Estonia and is used by an estimated 4,500 people.

### Education

Main article: [Education in Estonia](/wiki/Education_in_Estonia "Education in Estonia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Tartu_%C3%9Clikooli_peahoone_2012.jpg/250px-Tartu_%C3%9Clikooli_peahoone_2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tartu_%C3%9Clikooli_peahoone_2012.jpg)

The University of Tartu is one of the oldest universities in Northern Europe and the highest-ranked university in Estonia

In education, Estonia ranks as one of the top-performing countries of the world. According to the 2018 [PISA](/wiki/Programme_for_International_Student_Assessment "Programme for International Student Assessment") report, Estonian students placed 1st in Europe and globally ranked 5th in reading, 8th in mathematics, and 4th in sciences.[[361]](#cite_note-LoC-365)[[362]](#cite_note-366) Estonia also boasts one of the highest adult education levels in the [developed world](/wiki/Developed_country "Developed country"), with 89% of adults aged 25–64 having completed at least a high school degree.[[363]](#cite_note-367) The [University of Tartu](/wiki/University_of_Tartu "University of Tartu"), the nation's oldest university, has ranked 285th globally according to the QS World University Rankings.[[364]](#cite_note-368)

The roots of formal education in Estonia can be traced back to the 13th and 14th centuries, with the establishment of the first [monastic](/wiki/Monastic "Monastic") and [cathedral schools](/wiki/Cathedral_schools "Cathedral schools").[[365]](#cite_note-369) The publication of the first Estonian-language primer in 1575 further contributed to the development of education. The University of Tartu, founded in 1632, played a central role in higher education, with courses offered in Estonian for the first time in 1919. Following the restoration of independence in the 1990s, Estonia offered free public education in Russian, but in 2024, the country began transitioning all public schools to Estonian-only instruction, underscoring a renewed focus on the national language and culture.[[366]](#cite_note-370)

The Estonian education system is structured into four levels: pre-school, basic, secondary, and higher education, with schools spanning general, vocational, and hobby-focused categories.[[367]](#cite_note-371) In addition to traditional state and municipal schools, the country supports a range of private and public educational institutions, totaling 514 schools as of 2023.[[368]](#cite_note-372) Estonia has been a pioneer in educational technology, launching the *[Tiigrihüpe](/wiki/Tiigrih%C3%BCpe "Tiigrihüpe")* programme to equip schools with computers and internet access, significantly advancing digital literacy and connectivity within the education sector.[[369]](#cite_note-373)

Estonian higher education follows a three-tier structure of bachelor's, master's, and doctoral degrees, with some integrated programmes combining bachelor's and master's levels.[[370]](#cite_note-EurydiceSummary-374) Estonian public universities, such as the University of Tartu, [Tallinn University of Technology](/wiki/Tallinn_University_of_Technology "Tallinn University of Technology"), [Tallinn University](/wiki/Tallinn_University "Tallinn University"), and [Estonian University of Life Sciences](/wiki/Estonian_University_of_Life_Sciences "Estonian University of Life Sciences"), enjoy considerable autonomy, including control over academic curricula, admissions criteria, budgets, and governance.[[371]](#cite_note-375) Estonia also has a mix of public and private universities, with the [Estonian Business School](/wiki/Estonian_Business_School "Estonian Business School") standing as the country's largest private institution.

## Culture

[Estonian culture](/wiki/Culture_of_Estonia "Culture of Estonia") emphasizes indigenous practices and can be said to have a strong connection to the land and community. Contemporary Estonian society is marked by a strong commitment to individual liberty, advocating for the principles of limited government, and resistance to central power and corruption. The [Protestant work ethic](/wiki/Protestant_work_ethic "Protestant work ethic") remains a cultural mainstay, stressing diligence and self-reliance. Education is highly valued in Estonia, with free access to schooling being a highly prized institution. According to a study, Estonians are among the top in the world in terms of book ownership per capita.[[372]](#cite_note-376)

The cultural framework of Estonia reflects the [egalitarian](/wiki/Egalitarianism "Egalitarianism") ethos found in the [Nordic countries](/wiki/Nordic_countries "Nordic countries"), emerging from practical considerations such as [everyman's right](/wiki/Everyman%27s_right "Everyman's right") and [universal suffrage](/wiki/Universal_suffrage "Universal suffrage"), while also embodying ideals of closeness to nature.

As of 2023, Estonia boasted 170 museums, whose collections collectively hold over 10 million artefacts.[[373]](#cite_note-377)

One significant cultural tradition in Estonia is the [sauna](/wiki/Sauna "Sauna"), along with its accompanying rituals. The [smoke sauna](/wiki/Smoke_sauna "Smoke sauna") tradition of [Võru County](/wiki/V%C3%B5ru_County "Võru County"), distinguished by its lack of a chimney and the use of smoke in the heating process, was included in the [UNESCO](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") list of [intangible cultural heritage](/wiki/Intangible_cultural_heritage "Intangible cultural heritage") in 2014.[[374]](#cite_note-378)

Estonia observes 11 [public holidays](/wiki/Public_holidays_in_Estonia "Public holidays in Estonia") public holidays with mandatory days off and celebrates 12 additional national holidays each year.[[375]](#cite_note-379) The Estonian [National Day](/wiki/National_Day "National Day") is the [Independence Day](/wiki/Independence_Day_(Estonia) "Independence Day (Estonia)"), observed on 24 February to commemorate the 1918 Estonian Declaration of Independence. Another pivotal holiday, *[Võidupüha](/wiki/V%C3%B5idup%C3%BCha "Võidupüha")*, is celebrated on 23 June in remembrance of the Estonian victory in the 1918–1920 War of Independence. This holiday merges with the *[Jaanipäev](/wiki/Jaanip%C3%A4ev "Jaanipäev")* ([Midsummer Day](/wiki/Midsummer_Day "Midsummer Day")) on 24 June, one of the oldest and most widely celebrated events. On [Midsummer](/wiki/Midsummer "Midsummer") Eve, Estonians traditionally gather around [bonfires](/wiki/Bonfire "Bonfire") near the [village swing](/wiki/Village_swing "Village swing") to enjoy dancing, singing, and other festivities. [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas") (*[jõulud](/wiki/Yule "Yule")*) is also deeply cherished in Estonia, its traditions blending ancient [winter solstice](/wiki/Winter_solstice "Winter solstice") customs with Christian and modern holiday practices.[[376]](#cite_note-380)

### Music and dance

[Music of Estonia](/wiki/Music_of_Estonia "Music of Estonia") has a rich history that traces back to early mentions in medieval chronicles, with the first known reference appearing in [Saxo Grammaticus](/wiki/Saxo_Grammaticus "Saxo Grammaticus")' *Gesta Danorum* around 1179.[[377]](#cite_note-381) The oldest form of folk songs in Estonia, known as *regilaulud* ([runic songs](/wiki/Runic_song "Runic song")), followed a distinct poetic metre shared with other Baltic Finns.[[378]](#cite_note-382)

Estonian folk music also has a strong tradition of instrumental accompaniment, featuring the *[kannel](/wiki/Kannel_(instrument) "Kannel (instrument)")*, a zither-like instrument, along with the *[torupill](/wiki/Torupill "Torupill")*, an Estonian [bagpipe](/wiki/Bagpipe "Bagpipe"). The *torupill* was primarily used for dance music.[[379]](#cite_note-383) Other instruments, such as the [fiddle](/wiki/Fiddle "Fiddle"), [concertina](/wiki/Concertina "Concertina"), and [accordion](/wiki/Accordion "Accordion"), also contribute to the folk music repertoire, particularly for traditional dances. Medieval Estonian music reflects the influence of church music, with surviving liturgical manuscripts showcasing [early musical notation](/wiki/Mensural_notation "Mensural notation"). By the 14th century, organs were common in Estonian churches, while secular musicians played instruments like the *torupill* and drums at community gatherings.[[380]](#cite_note-384)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Laulupidu_07072019.jpg/250px-Laulupidu_07072019.jpg)](/wiki/File:Laulupidu_07072019.jpg)

The [Estonian Song Festival](/wiki/Estonian_Song_Festival "Estonian Song Festival") is one of the [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [Masterpieces of the Oral and Intangible Heritage of Humanity](/wiki/Masterpieces_of_the_Oral_and_Intangible_Heritage_of_Humanity "Masterpieces of the Oral and Intangible Heritage of Humanity") (XXVII Song Festival in 2019).

First held in 1869, the nationwide [Estonian Song Festival](/wiki/Estonian_Song_Festival "Estonian Song Festival") is the country's most notable musical traditions. Held every five years at the [Tallinn Song Festival Grounds](/wiki/Tallinn_Song_Festival_Grounds "Tallinn Song Festival Grounds"), the festival regularly draws up to 100,000 participants.[[381]](#cite_note-385)

Estonian composer [Arvo Pärt](/wiki/Arvo_P%C3%A4rt "Arvo Pärt") has achieved global acclaim for his minimalist, deeply spiritual works. Pärt became the world's most performed living composer from 2010 to 2018.[[382]](#cite_note-386)

[Estonian rock](/wiki/Rock_music_in_Estonia "Rock music in Estonia") and pop music emerged in the mid-1960s, with early bands performing in underground scenes to avoid scrutiny by Soviet occupation authorities. After regaining independence, Estonia won the [Eurovision Song Contest](/wiki/Eurovision_Song_Contest "Eurovision Song Contest") in 2001 with "[Everybody](/wiki/Everybody_(Eurovision_song) "Everybody (Eurovision song)")" (performed by [Tanel Padar](/wiki/Tanel_Padar "Tanel Padar") and [Dave Benton](/wiki/Dave_Benton "Dave Benton")).

[Estonian folk dance](/wiki/Estonian_folk_dance "Estonian folk dance") is characterised by repetitive motifs and simple movement patterns. Earliest written records of Estonian folk dance date to the 12th century. Elements from these ancient ritual dances are preserved in circle and chain dances, some of the oldest forms in Estonian dance.[[383]](#cite_note-387)[[384]](#cite_note-388) The nationwide [Estonian Dance Festival](/wiki/Estonian_Dance_Festival "Estonian Dance Festival") is held once every four years in Tallinn.[[385]](#cite_note-389)

### Mythology and folklore

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Kalevipoja_s%C3%B5it_P%C3%B5hjamaale_1935_Raud.jpg/250px-Kalevipoja_s%C3%B5it_P%C3%B5hjamaale_1935_Raud.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kalevipoja_s%C3%B5it_P%C3%B5hjamaale_1935_Raud.jpg)

[Kristjan Raud](/wiki/Kristjan_Raud "Kristjan Raud"), *Journey of [Kalevipoeg](/wiki/Kalevipoeg "Kalevipoeg") to the North*, 1935, [Estonian Art Museum](/wiki/Estonian_Art_Museum "Estonian Art Museum")

The [Estonian folklore](/wiki/Estonian_folklore "Estonian folklore") and [mythology](/wiki/Estonian_mythology "Estonian mythology") are believed to be rooted in pre-Christian beliefs.[[386]](#cite_note-390) While much of Estonia's ancient mythology is scattered across fragments of oral tradition, some of the oldest myths are believed to have survived in traditional [runic songs](/wiki/Runic_song "Runic song"). One such song narrates the birth of the world, where a bird lays three eggs, from which emerge the [Sun](/wiki/Sun "Sun"), the [Moon](/wiki/Moon "Moon"), and the [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth").[[387]](#cite_note-EACBS-391)

The mythical heroes and giants, such as [Kalevipoeg](/wiki/Kalevipoeg "Kalevipoeg") and [Suur Tõll](/wiki/Toell_the_Great "Toell the Great"), are central to Estonian folklore. Kalevipoeg, a mythological giant hero, is often portrayed defending the land from invaders, and many natural landmarks are said to be traces of his deeds. "The Dawn and Dusk" (*Koit ja Hämarik*) is considered one of the most beautiful Estonian myths with authentic origin.[[388]](#cite_note-392) [Jakob Hurt](/wiki/Jakob_Hurt "Jakob Hurt") launched a nationwide folklore collection campaign in the 1880s, thanks to which 12,400 pages of folklore were collected by volunteers. Inspired by Hurt, [Matthias Johann Eisen](/wiki/Matthias_Johann_Eisen "Matthias Johann Eisen") amassed a monumental 90,000 pages of folklore by the early 20th century. These collections are preserved by the [Estonian Folklore Archives](/wiki/Estonian_Folklore_Archives "Estonian Folklore Archives"), one of the largest such institutions in the world.[[389]](#cite_note-393)[[390]](#cite_note-394)

### Literature

Main articles: [Literature of Estonia](/wiki/Literature_of_Estonia "Literature of Estonia") and [Estonian poetry](/wiki/Estonian_poetry "Estonian poetry")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Anton_Hansen_Tammsaare%2C_000290.jpg/250px-Anton_Hansen_Tammsaare%2C_000290.jpg)](/wiki/File:Anton_Hansen_Tammsaare,_000290.jpg)

Writer [A. H. Tammsaare](/wiki/A._H._Tammsaare "A. H. Tammsaare") (1878–1940) whose pentalogy *[Truth and Justice](/wiki/Truth_and_Justice "Truth and Justice")* is considered "The Estonian Novel"

The oldest records of written Estonian date from the 13th century.[[391]](#cite_note-DWE-395) Written Estonian poetry emerged during 17th–18th centuries, with authors such as [Reiner Brockmann](/wiki/Reiner_Brockmann "Reiner Brockmann") and [Käsu Hans](/wiki/K%C3%A4su_Hans "Käsu Hans"). Despite this, few notable works of literature were written until the 19th century and the beginning of an Estonian national awakening. [Kristjan Jaak Peterson](/wiki/Kristjan_Jaak_Peterson "Kristjan Jaak Peterson"), the first renowned Estonian poet, emerged in the early 19th century, inspiring figures such as [Friedrich Robert Faehlmann](/wiki/Friedrich_Robert_Faehlmann "Friedrich Robert Faehlmann") and [Friedrich Reinhold Kreutzwald](/wiki/Friedrich_Reinhold_Kreutzwald "Friedrich Reinhold Kreutzwald") to preserve Estonian folk poetry and produce the *[Kalevipoeg](/wiki/Kalevipoeg "Kalevipoeg")*, the Estonian [national epic](/wiki/National_epic "National epic").[[392]](#cite_note-396) It is written in the [Kalevala tetrameter](/wiki/Kalevala_meter "Kalevala meter"), a largely lyrical form of folk poetry based on syllabic quantity.[[393]](#cite_note-397) The national awakening also spurred the rise of national romantic poetry, with [Lydia Koidula](/wiki/Lydia_Koidula "Lydia Koidula") as its foremost figure.[[394]](#cite_note-398)

The end of 1800s saw a rise of many poets and novelists who wrote in Estonian, notably [Juhan Liiv](/wiki/Juhan_Liiv "Juhan Liiv"), [August Kitzberg](/wiki/August_Kitzberg "August Kitzberg") and [Eduard Vilde](/wiki/Eduard_Vilde "Eduard Vilde"). Around 1905, a [neo-romantic](/wiki/Neo-romanticism "Neo-romanticism") literary movement [Young Estonia](/wiki/Young_Estonia "Young Estonia") (*Noor-Eesti*) led by poet [Gustav Suits](/wiki/Gustav_Suits "Gustav Suits") and including writers [Friedebert Tuglas](/wiki/Friedebert_Tuglas "Friedebert Tuglas"), [Villem Grünthal-Ridala](/wiki/Villem_Gr%C3%BCnthal-Ridala "Villem Grünthal-Ridala"), and [Johannes Aavik](/wiki/Johannes_Aavik "Johannes Aavik") amongst others, had an aesthetic programme that followed the trends of Finnish, French, German, Scandinavian and Italian literature of the time, comprising elements of [Impressionism](/wiki/Impressionism "Impressionism"), [Symbolism](/wiki/Symbolism_(arts) "Symbolism (arts)") and [Expressionism](/wiki/Expressionism "Expressionism"). [Oskar Luts](/wiki/Oskar_Luts "Oskar Luts") was an early 20th century prose writer whose works, especially the lyrical school novel *Kevade* (Spring), remain popular to this day.[[395]](#cite_note-399) In the early 20th century, Estonian poetry gained new depth with the [Siuru](/wiki/Siuru "Siuru") group, a literary movement embracing modernism and sensuality; its members included [Marie Under](/wiki/Marie_Under "Marie Under"), [Henrik Visnapuu](/wiki/Henrik_Visnapuu "Henrik Visnapuu"), and [Friedebert Tuglas](/wiki/Friedebert_Tuglas "Friedebert Tuglas"). The 1930s saw the emergence of [Arbujad](/wiki/Arbujad "Arbujad"), a group of poets known for their new, introspective and philosophical style.[[396]](#cite_note-400)

In the independent Estonia of the 1920s and 1930s, literature flourished, with highly regarded prose works by authors like [A. H. Tammsaare](/wiki/A._H._Tammsaare "A. H. Tammsaare") and [Karl Ristikivi](/wiki/Karl_Ristikivi "Karl Ristikivi") shaping the era. Tammsaare's social epic and psychological realist [pentalogy](/wiki/Pentalogy "Pentalogy"), *[Truth and Justice](/wiki/Truth_and_Justice "Truth and Justice")*, captured the evolution of Estonian society from a poor farmer community to an independent nation while following man's everlasting struggle with existential questions.[[397]](#cite_note-401)[[398]](#cite_note-402) Many writers fled Estonia in 1944 from Soviet rule. Between 1944 and 1990, exiled writers published 267 novels, 181 poetry collections, and 155 memoirs, reflecting their determination to preserve national identity.[[399]](#cite_note-403)

During the Soviet occupation, [Jaan Kross](/wiki/Jaan_Kross "Jaan Kross") became Estonia's best-known and most-translated writer, as he skillfully used historical allegory to convey the resilience of Estonian identity.[[400]](#cite_note-404)[[401]](#cite_note-405) [Jaan Kaplinski](/wiki/Jaan_Kaplinski "Jaan Kaplinski") and [Mats Traat](/wiki/Mats_Traat "Mats Traat") also held a significant place in then Estonian literature.[[402]](#cite_note-406)

Among the most popular writers of the late 20th and early 21st centuries are [Tõnu Õnnepalu](/wiki/T%C3%B5nu_%C3%95nnepalu "Tõnu Õnnepalu") and [Andrus Kivirähk](/wiki/Andrus_Kivir%C3%A4hk "Andrus Kivirähk"), who uses elements of Estonian folklore and mythology, deforming them into the absurd and grotesque.[[403]](#cite_note-407)

Estonian philosophy reflects a unique worldview and national identity, accentuating interpretations grounded in original Estonian terms rather than Latin or Germanic loanwords. This approach is based on the idea, championed by Estonian thinkers like [Uku Masing](/wiki/Uku_Masing "Uku Masing"), that philosophical concepts [derive meaning](/wiki/Linguistic_relativity "Linguistic relativity") from their [etymology](/wiki/Etymology "Etymology") and cultural context. Additionally, Estonian philosophy incorporates semiotic elements, influenced by the works of [Jakob von Uexküll](/wiki/Jakob_von_Uexk%C3%BCll "Jakob von Uexküll") and [Juri Lotman](/wiki/Juri_Lotman "Juri Lotman").[[404]](#cite_note-408)[[405]](#cite_note-409)[[406]](#cite_note-410)

### Art

Main article: [Estonian art](/wiki/Estonian_art "Estonian art")

Traces of Estonian artistry date back to the Stone Age, with decorated bone artefacts, [amber](/wiki/Amber "Amber") pendants, and early figurines. During the Middle Ages, [Gothic art](/wiki/Gothic_art "Gothic art") became prominent, visible in the medieval churches of Saaremaa and exemplified by [Bernt Notke](/wiki/Bernt_Notke "Bernt Notke")'s *[Danse Macabre](/wiki/Danse_Macabre_(Notke) "Danse Macabre (Notke)")* in [St. Nicholas Church, Tallinn](/wiki/St._Nicholas_Church,_Tallinn "St. Nicholas Church, Tallinn").[[407]](#cite_note-411) Renaissance painter [Michael Sittow](/wiki/Michael_Sittow "Michael Sittow"), trained in the [Early Netherlandish style](/wiki/Early_Netherlandish_painting "Early Netherlandish painting"), was Estonia's first internationally recognised artist.[[408]](#cite_note-412)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Eesti_pruut_%28Eesti_m%C3%B5rsja%29%2C_Gustav_Adolf_Hippius%2C_EKM_j_153-47_M_41.jpg/250px-Eesti_pruut_%28Eesti_m%C3%B5rsja%29%2C_Gustav_Adolf_Hippius%2C_EKM_j_153-47_M_41.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eesti_pruut_(Eesti_m%C3%B5rsja),_Gustav_Adolf_Hippius,_EKM_j_153-47_M_41.jpg)

[Gustav Adolf Hippius](/wiki/Gustav_Adolf_Hippius "Gustav Adolf Hippius"), *An Estonian bride*, 1852, [Art Museum of Estonia](/wiki/Art_Museum_of_Estonia "Art Museum of Estonia"), Tallinn

In the Neoclassical period, landscape painting gained prominence among Estonian artists, both at home and abroad. This era also saw the emergence of other genres like mural painting, miniature painting, glass painting, and watercolours, often featuring antique themes or Estonian nature scenes. Famous painters of this era include [Gustav Adolf Hippius](/wiki/Gustav_Adolf_Hippius "Gustav Adolf Hippius"), [Karl August Senff](/wiki/Karl_August_Senff "Karl August Senff") and [Julie Wilhelmine Hagen-Schwarz](/wiki/Julie_Wilhelmine_Hagen-Schwarz "Julie Wilhelmine Hagen-Schwarz").[[409]](#cite_note-413)[[410]](#cite_note-414) The 19th and early 20th centuries saw also a rise in national themes, led by painters like [Johann Köler](/wiki/Johann_K%C3%B6ler "Johann Köler"), who embraced Estonian landscapes and traditions.

During the 1918–1940 period of independent Estonia, artists increasingly integrated contemporary European avant-garde influences. The founding of the [Pallas Art School (1919–1940)](/wiki/Pallas_Art_School_(1919%E2%80%931940) "Pallas Art School (1919–1940)") in Tartu marked a turning point, giving rise to a generation of artists including [Konrad Mägi](/wiki/Konrad_M%C3%A4gi "Konrad Mägi"), [Nikolai Triik](/wiki/Nikolai_Triik "Nikolai Triik"), [Kristjan Raud](/wiki/Kristjan_Raud "Kristjan Raud"), and printmaker [Eduard Wiiralt](/wiki/Eduard_Wiiralt "Eduard Wiiralt"), whose works captured both the avant-garde spirit and uniquely Estonian sensibilities.[[411]](#cite_note-415)

When the Soviet army occupied Estonia in 1944, many Estonian artists fled westwards. Under the [Stalinist](/wiki/Stalinism "Stalinism") regime, art was heavily regulated, with [Socialist Realism](/wiki/Socialist_Realism "Socialist Realism") promoted as the official style, while Western influences were discouraged. However, by the 1960s, restrictions began to be relaxed. A breakthrough came with the formation of the [ANK '64](/wiki/ANK_%2764 "ANK '64") collective, a group of artists who broke from Soviet themes and embraced personal, fantastical worlds. Leading figures such as [Jüri Arrak](/wiki/J%C3%BCri_Arrak "Jüri Arrak") and [Tõnis Vint](/wiki/T%C3%B5nis_Vint "Tõnis Vint") explored modernist aesthetics, emphasising individual expression and imaginative realism. In 1966, the first Estonian modernist art works got the official permission from the Soviet authorities to be displayed in public exhibitions. By the 1970s, Estonian art had grown distinct from Moscow's official styles, embracing a modernism that highlighted personal vision and cultural identity.[[412]](#cite_note-416)

Since the 1990s, Estonian art has diversified significantly with the rise of photography, video, and conceptual art. This period saw the decline of centralised art funding and management, alongside the establishment of new media centres at the Estonian Academy of Arts. Artists such as [Toomas Vint](/wiki/Toomas_Vint "Toomas Vint") became known internationally, and Estonian video artists gained exposure in venues like the [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_Art_Biennial "São Paulo Art Biennial") and [Venice Biennales](/wiki/Venice_Biennale "Venice Biennale").[[413]](#cite_note-417)

[August Weizenberg](/wiki/August_Weizenberg "August Weizenberg") is considered one of the founders of Estonian sculpture. His eclectic style, primarily based on classicism, favoured marble. Another Estonian sculptor, [Amandus Adamson](/wiki/Amandus_Adamson "Amandus Adamson"), shifted towards a more relaxed style that incorporated elements of realism. [Anton Starkopf](/wiki/Anton_Starkopf "Anton Starkopf"), a prominent sculptor of the 1930s, developed a unique style using granite and explored diverse themes, including erotic undertones. The Soviet occupation halted the progress of sculpture in the 1940s. However, since the 1960s Estonian sculpture was able to increase output and use innovative approaches again.[[414]](#cite_note-418)

### Architecture

Main article: [Architecture of Estonia](/wiki/Architecture_of_Estonia "Architecture of Estonia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Mus%C3%A9e_de_plein_air_%28Tallinn%29_%287644656256%29.jpg/250px-Mus%C3%A9e_de_plein_air_%28Tallinn%29_%287644656256%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mus%C3%A9e_de_plein_air_(Tallinn)_(7644656256).jpg)

A traditional farmhouse built in the [Estonian vernacular style](/wiki/Estonian_vernacular_architecture "Estonian vernacular architecture")

The [architecture of Estonia](/wiki/Architecture_of_Estonia "Architecture of Estonia") reflects a blend of northern European styles shaped by local traditions and materials. The Estonian traditional rural architecture is distinguished by single farms set within open landscapes, typically including a separate [sauna](/wiki/Sauna "Sauna") building. [timber](/wiki/Lumber "Lumber") and [boulders](/wiki/Boulder "Boulder") have been used extensively in traditional Estonian structures. The *rehielamu* was a typical farmhouse style until the 19th century, a few examples of it still remain in some Estonian villages. Estonia is also home to many [hill forts](/wiki/Hill_forts "Hill forts") from pre-Christian times,[[415]](#cite_note-419)[[416]](#cite_note-420) medieval castles, churches, and countryside structures such as [manor houses](/wiki/List_of_palaces_and_manor_houses_in_Estonia "List of palaces and manor houses in Estonia"), [mills](/wiki/Gristmill "Gristmill"), and [inns](/wiki/Inn "Inn").[[417]](#cite_note-421)

Towns in medieval Estonia developed around [central marketplaces](/wiki/Town_square "Town square") with street networks forming cohesive "[old towns](/wiki/Old_town "Old town")". The earliest significant architectural trend was [Romanesque](/wiki/Romanesque_architecture "Romanesque architecture") in the 12th–13th centuries, evident in a few surviving examples, e.g [Valjala Church](/wiki/Valjala_Church "Valjala Church") in Saaremaa.[[418]](#cite_note-422) [Gothic architecture](/wiki/Gothic_architecture "Gothic architecture") beginning in the 13th century defined Estonia's medieval style, as seen in the [Kuressaare Castle](/wiki/Kuressaare_Castle "Kuressaare Castle"), [Hermann Castle](/wiki/Hermann_Castle "Hermann Castle") (Narva), [St. Mary's Cathedral, Tallinn](/wiki/St._Mary%27s_Cathedral,_Tallinn "St. Mary's Cathedral, Tallinn"), and the [Tartu Cathedral](/wiki/Tartu_Cathedral "Tartu Cathedral"). Geological differences influenced regional styles: South Estonia embraced red [brick Gothic](/wiki/Brick_Gothic "Brick Gothic"), while white [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone") dominated in Tallinn. The medieval [Tallinn Old Town](/wiki/Tallinn_Old_Town "Tallinn Old Town") is the country's most important architectural ensemble to this day.

In the late 19th and early 20th centuries, [National Romantic style](/wiki/National_Romantic_style "National Romantic style") emerged, seeking inspiration from traditional Estonian architecture. After 1918, the newly independent Estonia sought to express its identity through new buildings. The Estonian parliament building at [Toompea](/wiki/Toompea "Toompea"), designed by architects [Eugen Habermann](/wiki/Eugen_Habermann "Eugen Habermann") and [Herbert Johanson](/wiki/Herbert_Johanson "Herbert Johanson") and completed in 1922,[[419]](#cite_note-423) combines a traditionalist exterior with a unique [Expressionist](/wiki/Expressionist_architecture "Expressionist architecture") interior, making it the world's only Expressionist parliament building.[[420]](#cite_note-424) During the 1930s, Estonia saw the development of a distinctive style of [stripped Classicism](/wiki/Stripped_Classicism "Stripped Classicism"), influenced by architects such as [Alar Kotli](/wiki/Alar_Kotli "Alar Kotli") and [Edgar Johan Kuusik](/wiki/Edgar_Johan_Kuusik "Edgar Johan Kuusik"). Meanwhile, [Functionalism](/wiki/Functionalism_(architecture) "Functionalism (architecture)") gained popularity in [Nõmme](/wiki/N%C3%B5mme "Nõmme") and Pärnu, particularly through the work of [Olev Siinmaa](/wiki/Olev_Siinmaa "Olev Siinmaa").[[421]](#cite_note-425)

Under the 1944-1991 Soviet occupation, the architecture was heavily influenced by [Soviet urban planning](/wiki/Urban_planning_in_communist_countries "Urban planning in communist countries"). The 1980s marked a resurgence of Estonian architectural identity, with postmodernist architects drawing from 1930s styles. Since regaining independence, Estonia has embraced diverse architectural trends, especially in Tallinn's business districts. Architect [Vilen Künnapu](/wiki/Vilen_K%C3%BCnnapu "Vilen Künnapu") has emerged as a leading figure.[[422]](#cite_note-426)

### Theatre, cinema and animation

Main articles: [Theatre of Estonia](/wiki/Theatre_of_Estonia "Theatre of Estonia") and [Cinema of Estonia](/wiki/Cinema_of_Estonia "Cinema of Estonia")

See also: [List of Estonian films](/wiki/List_of_Estonian_films "List of Estonian films")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/a/a8/Tallinn_asv2022-04_img41_National_Opera.jpg/250px-Tallinn_asv2022-04_img41_National_Opera.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tallinn_asv2022-04_img41_National_Opera.jpg)

The [Estonia Theatre](/wiki/Estonia_Theatre "Estonia Theatre"), constructed in 1913 in Tallinn, now houses the [National Opera](/wiki/Estonian_National_Opera "Estonian National Opera") and [Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Estonian_National_Symphony_Orchestra "Estonian National Symphony Orchestra").

Estonian theatre has a long and evolving history, with the earliest recorded performances dating back to the 16th century. By 1784, the German playwright [August von Kotzebue](/wiki/August_von_Kotzebue "August von Kotzebue") helped establish an amateur theatre in Tallinn, and five years later, the Estonian language made its debut on stage in Kotzebue's play *The Father's Expectation*. The [Tallinn City Theatre](/wiki/Tallinna_saksa_teater "Tallinna saksa teater"), opened in 1809, became Estonia's first professional theatre, performing German but also some Estonian-language works.[[423]](#cite_note-427) However, a distinctly Estonian theatre culture began to take shape with the development of song and drama societies, including the landmark 1870 performance of [Lydia Koidula](/wiki/Lydia_Koidula "Lydia Koidula")'s *Cousin from Saaremaa* by the [Vanemuine Cultural Society](/wiki/Vanemuine_Cultural_Society "Vanemuine Cultural Society"), which marked the birth of Estonian national theatre.[[424]](#cite_note-428)

The early 20th century saw the professionalisation of Estonian theatre, with the [Vanemuine](/wiki/Vanemuine "Vanemuine") in Tartu and the [Estonia Theatre](/wiki/Estonia_(organization) "Estonia (organization)") in Tallinn formally becoming professional institutions in 1906. Led by director [Karl Menning](/wiki/Karl_Menning "Karl Menning"), Vanemuine began to stage high-quality productions that emphasised [naturalism](/wiki/Naturalism_(theatre) "Naturalism (theatre)") and ensemble performance. Plays by Estonian writers [August Kitzberg](/wiki/August_Kitzberg "August Kitzberg"), [Oskar Luts](/wiki/Oskar_Luts "Oskar Luts") and [Eduard Vilde](/wiki/Eduard_Vilde "Eduard Vilde") were staged among world classics. Estonia Theatre, meanwhile, became known for its star actors, including [Theodor Altermann](/wiki/Theodor_Altermann "Theodor Altermann"), [Paul Pinna](/wiki/Paul_Pinna "Paul Pinna"), and [Erna Villmer](/wiki/Erna_Villmer "Erna Villmer"), as well as for its pioneering work in opera and operetta from 1908 onwards. The 1920s and 1930s brought further diversity to the theatre scene with the establishment of the Workers' Theatre in Tallinn, known for its social critique, and the experimental Morning Theatre, which explored expressionism. New regional theatres also appeared in Viljandi and Narva. A vibrant and multifaceted theatre tradition has continued through the period of Soviet control and into the modern era.[[425]](#cite_note-429)

The history of cinematographic film production in Estonia started in 1908 with a newsreel documenting a visit to Tallinn by then King [Gustav V of Sweden](/wiki/Gustav_V_of_Sweden "Gustav V of Sweden").[[426]](#cite_note-430) [Narrative filmmaking](/wiki/Narrative_film "Narrative film") soon followed, with early works such as *[Laenatud naene](/wiki/Laenatud_naene "Laenatud naene")* (1913) and *[Karujaht Pärnumaal](/wiki/Karujaht_P%C3%A4rnumaal "Karujaht Pärnumaal")* (1914) by [Johannes Pääsuke](/wiki/Johannes_P%C3%A4%C3%A4suke "Johannes Pääsuke"). Modern Estonian cinema often adapts major Estonian literary works, as seen in *[Truth and Justice](/wiki/Truth_and_Justice_(2019_film) "Truth and Justice (2019 film)")*, *[November](/wiki/November_(2017_film) "November (2017 film)")*, *[The Heart of the Bear](/wiki/The_Heart_of_the_Bear "The Heart of the Bear")*, *[Names in Marble](/wiki/Names_in_Marble_(film) "Names in Marble (film)")*, and *[Autumn Ball](/wiki/Autumn_Ball "Autumn Ball")*. Estonian cinema has gained international recognition, with *[Tangerines](/wiki/Tangerines_(film) "Tangerines (film)")* (2013) receiving nominations for both the [Academy Awards](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards") and [Golden Globes](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Awards "Golden Globe Awards").[[427]](#cite_note-431)[[428]](#cite_note-432) Notable foreign films shot in Estonia include *[Stalker](/wiki/Stalker_(1979_film) "Stalker (1979 film)")* and *[Tenet](/wiki/Tenet "Tenet")*.[[429]](#cite_note-433)[[430]](#cite_note-434)
Estonian animation began in the 1930s with *[Adventures of Juku the Dog](/wiki/Adventures_of_Juku_the_Dog "Adventures of Juku the Dog")*, a short film by [Voldemar Päts](/wiki/Voldemar_P%C3%A4ts "Voldemar Päts"). Animator [Priit Pärn](/wiki/Priit_P%C3%A4rn "Priit Pärn"), celebrated for his surreal and satirical style, gained international recognition with *[Breakfast on the Grass](/wiki/Breakfast_on_the_Grass "Breakfast on the Grass")* (1987), a profound critique of totalitarian society. More recently, Estonian studios and notable directors [Janno Põldma](/wiki/Janno_P%C3%B5ldma "Janno Põldma") and [Heiki Ernits](/wiki/Heiki_Ernits "Heiki Ernits") have remained highly regarded in the global animation industry.[[431]](#cite_note-435)

### Media

The [media of Estonia](/wiki/Media_of_Estonia "Media of Estonia") is characterised by a diverse array of outlets and notable press freedom. Media landscape includes numerous weekly newspapers and magazines, along with nine domestic television channels, and a variety of radio stations. Estonia consistently ranks among the top nations for press freedom, achieving 6th place on the [Press Freedom Index](/wiki/Press_Freedom_Index "Press Freedom Index") globally in 2024 and 8th place in 2023, according to [Reporters Without Borders](/wiki/Reporters_Without_Borders "Reporters Without Borders").[[432]](#cite_note-436)[[433]](#cite_note-437)[[434]](#cite_note-438) Two main news agencies operate in Estonia: the [Baltic News Service](/wiki/Baltic_News_Service "Baltic News Service") (BNS), a private news agency established in 1990 that covers the Baltic states, and ETV24, part of *[Eesti Rahvusringhääling](/wiki/Eesti_Rahvusringh%C3%A4%C3%A4ling "Eesti Rahvusringhääling")*, Estonia's publicly funded broadcaster. Established in 2007, *Eesti Rahvusringhääling* consolidates radio and television services previously provided by [Eesti Raadio](/wiki/Eesti_Raadio "Eesti Raadio") and [Eesti Televisioon](/wiki/Eesti_Televisioon "Eesti Televisioon") under the Estonian National Broadcasting Act.[[435]](#cite_note-439)[[436]](#cite_note-440)

Estonia issued its first private TV licences in 1992 and launched its first private radio station in 1990. *[Õnne 13](/wiki/%C3%95nne_13 "Õnne 13")*, Estonia's longest-running television series since its debut in 1993, portrays everyday lives of Estonian families through changing societal landscapes, remaining the most popular TV show in Estonia.[[437]](#cite_note-441) Estonian television and entertainment is marked by a unique blend of satire and cultural critique, [dark humour](/wiki/Black_comedy "Black comedy") and social commentary, often pushing the boundaries of what is considered acceptable.

### Cuisine

Main article: [Estonian cuisine](/wiki/Estonian_cuisine "Estonian cuisine")

Traditionally, Estonian food was based on what was available from local farms and the sea, a custom still evident in modern Estonian dishes. For centuries, hunting and fishing were integral to Estonian food culture, and while these are now enjoyed more as hobbies, locally sourced meats and fish remain central to traditional Estonian meals. Most common staples include [rye bread](/wiki/Rye_bread "Rye bread"), pork, potatoes, and dairy products. Estonians especially value fresh ingredients in spring and summer, incorporating berries, herbs, and vegetables, while winter meals often feature preserved jams, [pickled cucumbers](/wiki/Pickling "Pickling") and mushrooms. Fish plays a significant role: [Baltic herring](/wiki/Baltic_herring "Baltic herring") (*räim*), along with [sprat](/wiki/European_sprat "European sprat") (*kilu*), are often served in spiced forms as appetisers or sandwiches, such as the popular *kiluvõileib*, an [open sandwich](/wiki/Open_sandwich "Open sandwich") with sprats on rye bread.[[438]](#cite_note-442)

A hallmark of Estonian meals is [black bread](/wiki/Black_bread "Black bread") made from rye, acclaimed for its rich flavour and dense texture, and served with almost every meal as an open sandwich base or an accompaniment to soups and main dishes. Cow dairy products hold an important place in Estonian cuisine, with milk, and its derivatives valued both as beverages and culinary ingredients.

Alcoholic beverages in Estonia are traditionally beer-based, with locally brewed [beers](/wiki/Beer "Beer") being the preferred drink to accompany meals. Estonian [fruit wines](/wiki/Fruit_wine "Fruit wine") made from apples and berries are also enjoyed alongside [vodka](/wiki/Vodka "Vodka") (*viin*) and other distilled spirits.[[439]](#cite_note-443)

### Sports

Main article: [Sport in Estonia](/wiki/Sport_in_Estonia "Sport in Estonia")

Since 1920, Estonia has [participated](/wiki/Estonia_at_the_Olympics "Estonia at the Olympics") at the Olympic Games and earned medals in athletics, cross-country and freestyle skiing, weightlifting, wrestling, rowing, fencing, cycling and other sports. Estonia's best Olympic rankings were 12th at the 2006 Winter Olympics and 14th at the 1936 Summer Olympics. With the total number of [all-time Olympic medals](/wiki/All-time_Olympic_Games_medal_table "All-time Olympic Games medal table") won (44) in relation to its population (1.4 million) as of 2025, Estonia is the 12th most successful country in the world in terms of accumulated Olympic medals per capita.[[440]](#cite_note-444)

Estonia maintains extensive indoor and outdoor facilities for a range of sports.[[441]](#cite_note-445) Popular team sports include [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball"), [volleyball](/wiki/Volleyball "Volleyball"), and [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"). A unique contribution to global sports from Estonia is [kiiking](/wiki/Kiiking "Kiiking").[[442]](#cite_note-446)
